> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET833 - NUMERICAL TECHNIQUES ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Euler Method, Modified Euler Method ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Euler's Method** is a first-order explicit single-step numerical procedure used to approximate the solution of an Initial Value Problem (IVP) of the form > $$\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y), \quad y(x_0) = y_0$$ > by advancing the solution from $x_n$ to $x_{n+1}$ using a straight-line approximation of the slope at the current point. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modified Euler's Method** (also called **Heun's Method** or the **Improved Euler / Predictor-Corrector Euler Method**) is a second-order single-step numerical scheme. It enhances the accuracy of the basic Euler method by averaging the slope at the beginning and the (predicted) slope at the end of each sub-interval. --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture) Imagine you are hiking down a mountain and your GPS only tells you the **slope (steepness)** at the spot you are currently standing on â€” it cannot "see ahead" of you. - **Euler's Method** is like saying: *"Take one small step of size $h$ in the direction of the slope I am currently standing on."* The path becomes a small straight line segment following the current slope. The problem: the slope in reality changes along that step, so you accumulate an error by assuming it is constant. - **Modified Euler's Method** is like saying: *"First, take a small tentative step using the current slope. Now look at the new slope at that tentative destination. Finally, walk using the **average** of the starting slope and the tentative-end slope."* This average (trapezoidal-like) estimate is much closer to the true curved path. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Euler uses **one slope** (rectangle rule for area). Modified Euler uses the **average of two slopes** (trapezoidal rule for area). That is why Modified Euler is **more accurate by one order of $h$**. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notation | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | $h$ | Step size (uniform) | $h = (x_n - x_0)/n$ | | $x_0, y_0$ | Initial condition | Given in the IVP | | $f(x, y)$ | Slope function | Derived from ODE | | $n$ | Number of steps | Integer, $n \ge 1$ | | $\varepsilon_T$ | Local truncation error | Euler: $O(h^2)$, Modified Euler: $O(h^3)$ | | $\varepsilon_G$ | Global error | Euler: $O(h)$, Modified Euler: $O(h^2)$ | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic â€” Euler's Method (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Given** the IVP: $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ with $y(x_0) = y_0$. 2. **Choose** a uniform step size $h$ and a target point $x = x_{\text{target}}$. 3. **Compute** the slope at the current point: $m_n = f(x_n, y_n)$. 4. **Approximate** the new $y$-value as: $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$. 5. **Update** $x$: $x_{n+1} = x_n + h$. 6. **Repeat** steps 3â€“5 until $x_n$ reaches the desired target. > **The 'Why' behind it:** The true $y(x_n + h)$ lies on a smooth curve. Replacing the curve locally with a straight line whose slope equals the derivative at the *starting* point gives a first-order Taylor-series truncation. This is geometrically equivalent to the **rectangle rule of numerical integration**. --- ### 2.2 Operational Logic â€” Modified Euler's Method (Predictor-Corrector) 1. **Predict** an intermediate value at $x_{n+1}$ using the basic Euler formula (a cheap "trial" step). 2. **Correct** this trial by replacing the Euler slope with the **arithmetic mean** of the slope at the start and the slope at the predicted end. 3. **Accept** the corrected value as $y_{n+1}$. 4. **Advance** $x$ and repeat. > **The 'Why' behind it:** The mean of the start-slope and the end-slope is exactly the **trapezoidal rule** applied to integrate $f(x, y)$ over $[x_n, x_{n+1}]$. Trapezoidal rule is more accurate than the rectangle rule, hence the order upgrade from 1 to 2. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table) | # | Method | Predictor (if any) | Corrector / Update Formula | Local Trunc. Error | Global Error | Order | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Euler's Method** | â€” | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$ | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h)$ | **1** | | 2 | **Modified Euler (Heun's)** | $y_{n+1}^{p} = y_n + h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$ | $y_{n+1} = y_n + \dfrac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1}^{p})\bigr]$ | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ | **2** | | 3 | Mid-Point Euler (alt. form) | $k_1 = h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$ | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\, y_n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right)$ | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ | **2** | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU 2024 scheme examiner's key, when "Modified Euler" is asked, the **Predictorâ€“Corrector (Heun's) form** is the default expected answer. The Mid-Point form is acceptable as a valid alternative. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mechanical Vibrations & Dynamics:** Solving $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ where the forcing function $F(t)$ has no closed-form integral. - **Control Systems:** Simulating step-response of non-linear plant models $\dot{y} = f(y, u)$. - **Thermal Systems:** Transient heat-conduction where $T(t)$ follows a non-linear ODE. - **Aerospace (Trajectory):** Real-time on-board integration of velocity/acceleration data where analytic solutions are infeasible. - **Chemical Reaction Engineering:** Solving rate equations for non-linear kinetics $dC/dt = -kC^n$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Method (from Taylor's Series) **Starting Point:** Taylor's expansion of $y(x)$ about the point $x_n$: $$ y(x_n + h) = y(x_n) + h\,y'(x_n) + \frac{h^2}{2!}y''(x_n) + \frac{h^3}{3!}y'''(x_n) + \cdots $$ Since $y'(x_n) = f(x_n, y(x_n))$, substitute: $$ y(x_n + h) = y(x_n) + h\,f(x_n, y(x_n)) + \frac{h^2}{2!}y''(x_n) + \frac{h^3}{3!}y'''(x_n) + \cdots $$ **Truncation Step:** Discarding all terms of order $h^2$ and higher: $$ y(x_n + h) \approx y(x_n) + h\,f(x_n, y(x_n)) $$ **Discretization Step:** Replacing the exact values $y(x_n), y(x_{n+1})$ with numerical approximations $y_n, y_{n+1}$: $$ \boxed{\,y_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n)\,} $$ **Local Truncation Error (LTE):** The leading discarded term is $\dfrac{h^2}{2!}y''(x_n)$, so the LTE per step is $$ \tau_{n+1} = \frac{h^2}{2} y''(\xi_n), \quad \xi_n \in (x_n, x_{n+1}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad O(h^2) $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Modified Euler's Method (Trapezoidal Approach) **Conceptual Starting Point:** The integral form of the ODE between $x_n$ and $x_{n+1}$ is $$ y(x_{n+1}) - y(x_n) = \int_{x_n}^{x_{n+1}} f(x, y(x))\,dx $$ **Approximation using the Trapezoidal Rule** for the integral on the right: $$ \int_{x_n}^{x_{n+1}} f(x, y(x))\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\Bigl[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1})\Bigr] $$ Substituting this approximation into the integral equation: $$ y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\Bigl[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1})\Bigr] $$ **The Implicit Issue:** The unknown $y_{n+1}$ appears **inside** the function $f$ on the right side. This is an **implicit** formula and cannot be used directly. **Resolution via Predictor-Corrector:** - **Predictor (explicit Euler):** Use a quick explicit estimate for $y_{n+1}$: $$ y_{n+1}^{p} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n) $$ - **Corrector (trapezoidal with predicted end slope):** Plug the predictor into the RHS of the trapezoidal formula: $$ \boxed{\,y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\Bigl[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1},\, y_{n+1}^{p})\Bigr]\,} $$ **Order of the Method:** Because the trapezoidal rule has a local truncation error of $O(h^3)$, Modified Euler becomes a **second-order** method with global error $O(h^2)$. --- ### 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example (Standard 14-Mark Type) **Problem:** Solve $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y$ with $y(0) = 1$. Find $y(0.2)$ using step size $h = 0.1$, by: - (a) Euler's Method - (b) Modified Euler's Method > Compare with the exact solution $y(x) = 2e^{x} - x - 1$, so $y(0.2) = 2e^{0.2} - 1.2 \approx 1.24281$. --- #### (a) Euler's Method â€” Step-by-Step Computation Given: $f(x, y) = x + y$, $\;x_0 = 0$, $\;y_0 = 1$, $\;h = 0.1$. **Step 1: From $x_0 = 0$ to $x_1 = 0.1$** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x_0, y_0) &= f(0, 1) = 0 + 1 = 1 \\ y_1 &= y_0 + h \cdot f(x_0, y_0) = 1 + (0.1)(1) = 1.1000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: From $x_1 = 0.1$ to $x_2 = 0.2$** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x_1, y_1) &= f(0.1,\; 1.1000) = 0.1 + 1.1000 = 1.2000 \\ y_2 &= y_1 + h \cdot f(x_1, y_1) = 1.1000 + (0.1)(1.2000) = 1.2200 \end{aligned} $$ > **Euler's Result:** $\,y(0.2) \approx 1.2200$ > **Absolute Error:** $\vert 1.24281 - 1.2200 \vert = 0.02281$ --- #### (b) Modified Euler's Method â€” Step-by-Step Computation **Step 1: From $x_0 = 0$ to $x_1 = 0.1$** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Predictor: } y_1^{p} &= y_0 + h \cdot f(x_0, y_0) = 1 + (0.1)(1) = 1.1000 \\[4pt] f(x_0, y_0) &= f(0, 1) = 1 \\ f(x_1, y_1^{p}) &= f(0.1,\; 1.1000) = 0.1 + 1.1000 = 1.2000 \\[4pt] \text{Corrector: } y_1 &= y_0 + \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_0, y_0) + f(x_1, y_1^{p})\bigr] \\ &= 1 + \frac{0.1}{2}\bigl[1 + 1.2000\bigr] \\ &= 1 + (0.05)(2.2000) = 1.1100 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: From $x_1 = 0.1$ to $x_2 = 0.2$** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Predictor: } y_2^{p} &= y_1 + h \cdot f(x_1, y_1) \\ &= 1.1100 + (0.1)(0.1 + 1.1100) \\ &= 1.1100 + (0.1)(1.2100) = 1.2310 \\[4pt] f(x_1, y_1) &= f(0.1,\; 1.1100) = 1.2100 \\ f(x_2, y_2^{p}) &= f(0.2,\; 1.2310) = 0.2 + 1.2310 = 1.4310 \\[4pt] \text{Corrector: } y_2 &= y_1 + \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_1, y_1) + f(x_2, y_2^{p})\bigr] \\ &= 1.1100 + (0.05)\bigl[1.2100 + 1.4310\bigr] \\ &= 1.1100 + (0.05)(2.6410) \\ &= 1.1100 + 0.13205 = 1.24205 \end{aligned} $$ > **Modified Euler's Result:** $\,y(0.2) \approx 1.24205$ > **Absolute Error:** $\vert 1.24281 - 1.24205 \vert = 0.00076$ --- ### 3.4 Side-by-Side Comparison (KTU 14-Mark Verdict Table) | $x_n$ | Exact $y$ | Euler $y_n$ | Modified Euler $y_n$ | Euler Error | Mod. Euler Error | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0.0 | 1.00000 | 1.00000 | 1.00000 | 0 | 0 | | 0.1 | 1.11034 | 1.10000 | 1.11000 | 0.01034 | 0.00034 | | 0.2 | 1.24281 | 1.22000 | 1.24205 | 0.02281 | 0.00076 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** Modified Euler's error is roughly **30 times smaller** than Euler's error at $x = 0.2$ â€” confirming the theoretical $O(h^2)$ vs. $O(h)$ global-error advantage. --- ### 3.5 Stability & Limitation Note > [!NOTE] > Euler's method can become **unstable** (oscillates or diverges) when applied to stiff equations or when $h$ exceeds a stability bound related to the eigenvalues of the Jacobian of $f$. Modified Euler, being second-order, is **more stable but not unconditionally stable** either. For stiff problems, implicit methods (e.g., backward Euler, trapezoidal) are preferred. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Euler's Method â€” Algorithmic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0([Start: Given dy/dx equals f of x and y, y of x0 equals y0]) --> A1[Read h and target x final] A1 --> A2[Set xn equals x0, yn equals y0, n equals 0] A2 --> A3[Compute slope m equals f of xn, yn] A3 --> A4[Update yn plus 1 equals yn plus h times m] A4 --> A5[Update xn plus 1 equals xn plus h] A5 --> A6[Increment n by 1] A6 --> A7{Is xn greater than or equal to x final?} A7 -- No --> A3 A7 -- Yes --> A8[Print yn as y of x final] A8 --> A9([End]) ``` ### 4.2 Modified Euler's Method â€” Predictor-Corrector Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD B0([Start: Given dy/dx equals f of x and y, y of x0 equals y0]) --> B1[Read h and target x final] B1 --> B2[Set xn equals x0, yn equals y0] B2 --> B3[Predictor: Compute y star equals yn plus h times f of xn, yn] B3 --> B4[Slope start equals f of xn, yn] B4 --> B5[Slope end equals f of xn plus 1, y star] B5 --> B6[Corrector: yn plus 1 equals yn plus h over 2 times slope start plus slope end] B6 --> B7[Update xn plus 1 equals xn plus h] B7 --> B8{Reached target x?} B8 -- No --> B3 B8 -- Yes --> B9[Print final yn] B9 --> B10([End]) ``` ### 4.3 Geometric Interpretation (Euler vs. Modified Euler on a Curve) ```mermaid flowchart LR C0[Start point: xn, yn] --> C1[Slope m1 equals f of xn, yn] C1 --> C2[Straight line of slope m1
Euler takes this tangent line] C2 --> C3[Euler endpoint: yn plus 1 = yn plus h times m1] C0 --> C4[Predict tentative point y star using m1] C4 --> C5[Evaluate slope m2 at predicted point] C5 --> C6[Average slope m average equals m1 plus m2 over 2] C6 --> C7[Modified Euler endpoint using m average] C3 --> C8([Euler has larger deviation from true curve]) C7 --> C9([Modified Euler tracks curve more closely]) ``` ### 4.4 Block Architecture â€” Position in the Numerical IVP Solver Family ```mermaid flowchart TD D0[Initial Value Problem dy/dx = f of x, y] --> D1{Choose Method Class} D1 -- Single step explicit --> D2[Lowest: Euler order 1] D1 -- Single step explicit --> D3[Improved: Modified Euler order 2] D1 -- Single step explicit --> D4[Higher: RK2, RK4 order 4] D1 -- Multi step --> D5[Adams Bashforth, Milne Simpson] D1 -- Implicit --> D6[Backward Euler, Trapezoidal] D2 --> D7([Foundation for all explicit ODE solvers]) D3 --> D8([Mid tier accuracy and stability]) D4 --> D9([Industry default RK4]) ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the formula for Euler's method to solve the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$, $y(x_0) = y_0$. Mention its order of accuracy.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The iteration formula is > $$y_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n), \quad x_{n+1} = x_n + h$$ > where $h$ is the step size. It is obtained by truncating the Taylor series of $y(x_n + h)$ after the first-order term. > **Order of accuracy:** Euler's method is a **first-order method**. Its local truncation error per step is $O(h^2)$ and its global (accumulated) error is $O(h)$. --- #### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Write the predictor-corrector formulae used in the Modified Euler's Method. Why is it called a second-order method?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Predictor (Euler's first guess):** > $$y_{n+1}^{p} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n)$$ > **Corrector (trapezoidal average):** > $$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1},\, y_{n+1}^{p})\bigr]$$ > It is called a **second-order method** because the corrector uses the trapezoidal approximation, whose leading error term is $\dfrac{h^3}{12}y'''(\xi)$. Hence local truncation error is $O(h^3)$ and global error is $O(h^2)$. --- ### 5.2 Part B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** â€” `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** Derive the Euler's formula for solving $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ with $y(x_0) = y_0$, starting from Taylor's series expansion. Mention the local truncation error. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using Euler's method, find $y(0.2)$ for the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x^2 + y$, $y(0) = 1$, taking $h = 0.1$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** From the Taylor expansion of $y(x)$ about $x_n$: $$ y(x_n + h) = y(x_n) + h\,y'(x_n) + \frac{h^2}{2!}y''(x_n) + \frac{h^3}{3!}y'''(x_n) + \cdots $$ Substituting $y'(x_n) = f(x_n, y(x_n))$: $$ y(x_n + h) = y(x_n) + h\,f(x_n, y(x_n)) + \frac{h^2}{2!}y''(x_n) + \frac{h^3}{3!}y'''(x_n) + \cdots $$ Retaining terms only up to $h^1$: $$ y(x_n + h) \approx y(x_n) + h\,f(x_n, y(x_n)) $$ Replacing exact $y(\cdot)$ values with numerical $y_n$ values: $$ \boxed{\,y_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n)\,} $$ The discarded first term is $\dfrac{h^2}{2!}y''(x_n)$, so the **local truncation error** is $$ \tau_{n+1} = \frac{h^2}{2}\,y''(\xi_n), \quad \xi_n \in (x_n, x_{n+1}) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; O(h^2) $$ **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** Given $f(x, y) = x^2 + y$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.1$. **Step 1: $x_0 = 0 \to x_1 = 0.1$** $$ \begin{aligned} f(0, 1) &= 0^2 + 1 = 1 \\ y_1 &= 1 + (0.1)(1) = 1.1000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: $x_1 = 0.1 \to x_2 = 0.2$** $$ \begin{aligned} f(0.1, 1.1000) &= (0.1)^2 + 1.1000 = 0.01 + 1.1000 = 1.1100 \\ y_2 &= 1.1000 + (0.1)(1.1100) = 1.1000 + 0.1110 = 1.2110 \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** $y(0.2) \approx 1.2110$ (Euler's Method) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** â€” `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the Modified Euler's method (Heun's method) as a predictor-corrector scheme. Derive its corrector formula from the trapezoidal rule. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using Modified Euler's method, compute $y(0.2)$ for the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x - y^2$, $y(0) = 0$, with $h = 0.1$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Integrating the ODE $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ from $x_n$ to $x_{n+1}$: $$ y(x_{n+1}) - y(x_n) = \int_{x_n}^{x_{n+1}} f(x, y(x))\,dx $$ Approximating the integral with the **trapezoidal rule**: $$ \int_{x_n}^{x_{n+1}} f(x, y(x))\,dx \approx \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1})\bigr] $$ Hence, $$ y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1})\bigr] $$ Since $y_{n+1}$ is unknown on the RHS, we first **predict** it with basic Euler: $$ y_{n+1}^{p} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n) $$ Then we **correct** by inserting the predictor into the trapezoidal formula: $$ \boxed{\,y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1},\, y_{n+1}^{p})\bigr]\,} $$ This pair of equations constitutes the **predictor-corrector** form of the Modified Euler's method. **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** Given $f(x, y) = x - y^2$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 0$, $h = 0.1$. **Step 1: $x_0 = 0 \to x_1 = 0.1$** $$ \begin{aligned} f(0, 0) &= 0 - 0^2 = 0 \\ y_1^{p} &= 0 + (0.1)(0) = 0.0000 \\ f(0.1, 0) &= 0.1 - 0^2 = 0.1000 \\ y_1 &= 0 + (0.05)\bigl[0 + 0.1000\bigr] = 0.0050 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: $x_1 = 0.1 \to x_2 = 0.2$** $$ \begin{aligned} f(0.1, 0.0050) &= 0.1 - (0.0050)^2 = 0.1 - 0.000025 = 0.099975 \\ y_2^{p} &= 0.0050 + (0.1)(0.099975) = 0.0050 + 0.0099975 = 0.0149975 \\ f(0.2, 0.0149975) &= 0.2 - (0.0149975)^2 = 0.2 - 0.00022493 \approx 0.19977507 \\ y_2 &= 0.0050 + (0.05)\bigl[0.099975 + 0.19977507\bigr] \\ &= 0.0050 + (0.05)(0.29975007) \\ &= 0.0050 + 0.01498750 \approx 0.01999 \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** $y(0.2) \approx 0.01999$ (Modified Euler's Method) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:** > 1. **Forgetting the predictor step:** Many students jump straight to the corrector and write $y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, y_n)]$ â€” losing **3 marks** for the missing predictor line. > 2. **Mis-updating $x$:** Always write $x_{n+1} = x_n + h$ before computing the corrector. Examiners deduct marks if $x_{n+1}$ is silently used without definition. > 3. **Rounding too early:** Round only the **final** answer to 4 decimals. Intermediate values must be kept to at least 6 decimals to avoid error propagation. > 4. **Confusing $h/2$ with $2h$:** The factor inside the corrector is **$\dfrac{h}{2}$** multiplying the sum of the two slopes â€” not $\dfrac{1}{2h}$ or $2h$. > 5. **Stopping at the wrong index:** For "find $y(0.2)$" with $h = 0.1$, you need **two** iterations ($n=0 \to n=1$ and $n=1 \to n=2$). Stopping at one iteration costs half the marks. > 6. **Wrong order claim:** Stating "Euler is second-order" or "Modified Euler is first-order" will lose the order-of-accuracy mark. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap â€” Rapid Revision Checklist - **Euler's method** advances the solution using only the slope at the **current** point: $y_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n)$. - **Order of Euler's method:** 1 (Local error $O(h^2)$, Global error $O(h)$). - **Modified Euler's method** uses a **predictor-corrector pair**; predictor is basic Euler, corrector uses the **average of start and end slopes**. - **Modified Euler corrector:** $y_{n+1} = y_n + \dfrac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1}^{p})\bigr]$. - **Order of Modified Euler:** 2 (Local error $O(h^3)$, Global error $O(h^2)$). - **Geometric meaning:** Euler = rectangle rule; Modified Euler = trapezoidal rule applied to the slope integral. - **Computational cost per step:** Modified Euler requires **2 evaluations** of $f$ per step (one for predictor, one for corrector), versus **1** for Euler â€” a trade-off of cost for accuracy. - **Stability:** Both methods are conditionally stable; reducing $h$ improves accuracy but increases computational load. - **Always** state initial conditions, step size, target, and final answer with **at least 4 decimal places** in KTU papers. - **For 14-mark questions,** examiners expect: (i) derivation with all Taylor / trapezoidal steps, (ii) clearly labelled predictor & corrector, (iii) iteration-wise computation, and (iv) a final comparison or comment on accuracy. - **Memory tip:** "*Euler uses **one** slope. Modified Euler uses **two** slopes and takes their **average** â€” that's why it is 'modified' and 'better'.*"

topics.title: Runge Kutta Fourth Order Method for First Order, Second Order and Simultaneous First Order Differential Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET833 - NUMERICAL TECHNIQUES ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Runge-Kutta Fourth Order Method for First Order, Second Order and Simultaneous First Order Differential Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

The **Runge-Kutta Fourth Order Method (RK4)** is an explicit, single-step numerical integration technique used to approximate the solution of an Initial Value Problem (IVP) of the form:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y), \quad y(x_0) = y_0$$

It belongs to the family of Runge-Kutta methods and computes the next value $y_{n+1}$ by taking a **weighted average of four strategically evaluated slopes** ($k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$) within the interval $[x_n, x_{n+1}]$. The "fourth order" indicates that the local truncation error is of the order $O(h^5)$ and the global error is of the order $O(h^4)$, where $h$ is the step size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RK4 is the gold standard for hand-computed single-step ODE solutions in KTU ESE papers. It offers an excellent trade-off between **accuracy** and **computational effort** without requiring higher derivatives of $f(x,y)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car along a winding mountain road from point A to point B. You cannot see the entire road at once. To estimate your direction:

- **$k_1$**: You peek at the road directly in front of you (slope at the start).
- **$k_2$**: You walk halfway forward and check the slope there.
- **$k_3$**: You re-evaluate the slope at the midpoint using your halfway estimate (more accurate preview).
- **$k_4$**: You leap to the end of the interval and check the slope at the final point.

Your final move (step forward by $h$) is a **weighted compromise** of all four "looks." By averaging these four different vantage points, you avoid blindly following a single bad slope. The weights $\frac{1}{6}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{6}$ give more importance to the midpoint estimates because they tend to be most representative of the average slope over the interval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step size $h$ = 0.1** or **$h$ = 0.2** are the most common choices in KTU question papers. Always confirm $h$ before starting the iteration.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown â€” The Algorithm Logic

The RK4 update equation for a first-order ODE is:

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$$

where the four intermediate slopes are computed as:

| Slope | Formula | Geometric Meaning |
|:-----:|:--------|:------------------|
| $k_1$ | $h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$ | Slope at the **start** of interval |
| $k_2$ | $h \cdot f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right)$ | Slope at the **midpoint**, using $k_1$ to predict |
| $k_3$ | $h \cdot f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right)$ | **Improved** midpoint slope, using $k_2$ |
| $k_4$ | $h \cdot f(x_n + h,\; y_n + k_3)$ | Slope at the **end** of interval |

### 2.2 Extension to Second Order ODEs

A second-order ODE $y'' = g(x, y, y')$ is converted into a **system of two simultaneous first-order ODEs** by introducing an auxiliary variable:

$$\text{Let } u = y, \quad v = y'$$

Then the system becomes:

$$\frac{du}{dx} = v, \quad \frac{dv}{dx} = g(x, u, v)$$

with initial conditions $u(x_0) = y(x_0)$ and $v(x_0) = y'(x_0)$.

### 2.3 Extension to Simultaneous First Order ODEs

For a system of $m$ simultaneous ODEs:

$$\frac{dy_1}{dx} = f_1(x, y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m)$$
$$\frac{dy_2}{dx} = f_2(x, y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m)$$
$$\vdots$$
$$\frac{dy_m}{dx} = f_m(x, y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m)$$

For each dependent variable $y_i$, four auxiliary constants ($k_i, l_i, m_i, \ldots$) are computed in parallel, all using the same $x$-increments.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bookmark this table. Every KTU RK4 problem will reduce to plugging values into these boxes.**

| Component | Formula | Notes |
|:----------|:--------|:------|
| Update (First Order) | $y_{n+1} = y_n + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$ | Core recurrence |
| $k_1$ (First Order) | $h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$ | Initial slope |
| $k_2$ (First Order) | $h \cdot f(x_n + h/2,\; y_n + k_1/2)$ | Midpoint slope (predict) |
| $k_3$ (First Order) | $h \cdot f(x_n + h/2,\; y_n + k_2/2)$ | Midpoint slope (correct) |
| $k_4$ (First Order) | $h \cdot f(x_n + h,\; y_n + k_3)$ | Endpoint slope |
| Update ($u$) | $u_{n+1} = u_n + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$ | Replace $y$ with $u$ |
| Update ($v$) | $v_{n+1} = v_n + \tfrac{1}{6}(l_1 + 2l_2 + 2l_3 + l_4)$ | For 2nd-order / systems |
| $l_1, l_2, l_3, l_4$ | Same pattern using $g(x, u, v)$ | $g$ is the second equation |
| Truncation Error | Local: $O(h^5)$, Global: $O(h^4)$ | Why it is "fourth order" |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|:------|:------------|
| Aerospace | Trajectory simulation of spacecraft and rockets |
| Electrical | Transient analysis of RLC circuits |
| Mechanical | Vibration analysis of spring-mass-damper systems |
| Biomedical | Population dynamics, drug concentration modeling |
| Control Systems | State-space simulation of plant dynamics |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 General Formula Pattern (Universal Form)

For any RK4 application, the following **master template** applies. Memorize this structure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= h \cdot f(x_n, y_n) \\
k_2 &= h \cdot f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right) \\
k_3 &= h \cdot f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right) \\
k_4 &= h \cdot f(x_n + h,\; y_n + k_3) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \frac{k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

The "+/- $h/2$" and "$+/- k/2$" come from evaluating the function at the **midpoint** of the current interval using a **first-order Euler estimate**.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 â€” First Order ODE (KTU Standard Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Given $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y$ with $y(0) = 1$, find $y(0.1)$ and $y(0.2)$ using RK4 with $h = 0.1$.

**Iteration 1: From $x_0 = 0$ to $x_1 = 0.1$**

Here $f(x, y) = x + y$. We have $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= h \cdot f(x_0, y_0) = 0.1 \cdot (0 + 1) = 0.1 \cdot 1 = 0.100000 \\
k_2 &= h \cdot f\!\left(x_0 + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_0 + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.05,\; 1.05) = 0.1 \cdot (0.05 + 1.05) = 0.1 \cdot 1.10 = 0.110000 \\
k_3 &= h \cdot f\!\left(x_0 + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_0 + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.05,\; 1.055) = 0.1 \cdot (0.05 + 1.055) = 0.1 \cdot 1.105 = 0.110500 \\
k_4 &= h \cdot f(x_0 + h,\; y_0 + k_3) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.1,\; 1.1105) = 0.1 \cdot (0.1 + 1.1105) = 0.1 \cdot 1.2105 = 0.121050
\end{aligned}
$$

Update $y_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= y_0 + \frac{k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{0.100000 + 2(0.110000) + 2(0.110500) + 0.121050}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{0.100000 + 0.220000 + 0.221000 + 0.121050}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{0.662050}{6} \\
    &= 1 + 0.110342 \\
    &= 1.110342
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2: From $x_1 = 0.1$ to $x_2 = 0.2$**

Now $x_1 = 0.1$, $y_1 = 1.110342$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= 0.1 \cdot (0.1 + 1.110342) = 0.1 \cdot 1.210342 = 0.121034 \\
k_2 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.15,\; 1.110342 + 0.060517) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot (0.15 + 1.170859) = 0.1 \cdot 1.320859 = 0.132086 \\
k_3 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.15,\; 1.110342 + 0.066043) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot (0.15 + 1.176385) = 0.1 \cdot 1.326385 = 0.132639 \\
k_4 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.2,\; 1.110342 + 0.132639) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot (0.2 + 1.242981) = 0.1 \cdot 1.442981 = 0.144298
\end{aligned}
$$

Update $y_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_2 &= y_1 + \frac{0.121034 + 2(0.132086) + 2(0.132639) + 0.144298}{6} \\
    &= 1.110342 + \frac{0.121034 + 0.264172 + 0.265278 + 0.144298}{6} \\
    &= 1.110342 + \frac{0.794782}{6} \\
    &= 1.110342 + 0.132464 \\
    &= 1.242806
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** $y(0.1) \approx 1.110342$, $y(0.2) \approx 1.242806$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 â€” Second Order ODE

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Solve $y'' + y = 0$ with $y(0) = 1$, $y'(0) = 0$ using RK4 with $h = 0.1$. Find $y(0.1)$.

**Step A: Reduce to First Order System**

Let $u = y$ and $v = y'$.

$$
\frac{du}{dx} = v, \quad \frac{dv}{dx} = -u
$$

Initial conditions: $u_0 = 1$, $v_0 = 0$ at $x_0 = 0$.

**Step B: Apply RK4 to Both Equations Simultaneously**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= h \cdot v_0 = 0.1 \cdot 0 = 0 \\
l_1 &= h \cdot (-u_0) = 0.1 \cdot (-1) = -0.100 \\
k_2 &= h \cdot \left(v_0 + \tfrac{l_1}{2}\right) = 0.1 \cdot (0 - 0.05) = -0.005 \\
l_2 &= h \cdot \left(-\!\left(u_0 + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right)\right) = 0.1 \cdot (-1) = -0.100 \\
k_3 &= h \cdot \left(v_0 + \tfrac{l_2}{2}\right) = 0.1 \cdot (-0.05) = -0.005 \\
l_3 &= h \cdot \left(-\!\left(u_0 + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right)\right) = 0.1 \cdot (-(1 - 0.0025)) = -0.099750 \\
k_4 &= h \cdot \left(v_0 + l_3\right) = 0.1 \cdot (-0.099750) = -0.009975 \\
l_4 &= h \cdot \left(-\!\left(u_0 + k_3\right)\right) = 0.1 \cdot (-(1 - 0.005)) = -0.099500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C: Update $u$ and $v$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= u_0 + \frac{k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{0 + 2(-0.005) + 2(-0.005) + (-0.009975)}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{-0.029975}{6} \\
    &= 1 - 0.004996 \\
    &= 0.995004 \\
\\
v_1 &= v_0 + \frac{l_1 + 2l_2 + 2l_3 + l_4}{6} \\
    &= 0 + \frac{-0.100 + 2(-0.100) + 2(-0.099750) + (-0.099500)}{6} \\
    &= \frac{-0.599000}{6} \\
    &= -0.099833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** $y(0.1) \approx 0.995004$, $y'(0.1) \approx -0.099833$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** Exact solution is $y = \cos(x)$. We have $\cos(0.1) = 0.995004 \checkmark$ and $-\sin(0.1) = -0.099833 \checkmark$. RK4 is essentially exact for this simple harmonic ODE at $h = 0.1$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 â€” Simultaneous First Order ODEs

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Solve $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + z$, $\dfrac{dz}{dx} = x - y$ with $y(0) = 1$, $z(0) = 2$, $h = 0.1$. Find $y(0.1)$, $z(0.1)$.

**Identification:** Here $f_1(x, y, z) = x + z$ and $f_2(x, y, z) = x - y$.

Initial values: $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $z_0 = 2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= h \cdot (x_0 + z_0) = 0.1 \cdot (0 + 2) = 0.200 \\
l_1 &= h \cdot (x_0 - y_0) = 0.1 \cdot (0 - 1) = -0.100 \\
k_2 &= h \cdot \left(x_0 + \tfrac{h}{2} + z_0 + \tfrac{l_1}{2}\right) = 0.1 \cdot (0.05 + 2 - 0.05) = 0.1 \cdot 2.00 = 0.200 \\
l_2 &= h \cdot \left(x_0 + \tfrac{h}{2} - y_0 - \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right) = 0.1 \cdot (0.05 - 1 - 0.10) = 0.1 \cdot (-1.05) = -0.105 \\
k_3 &= h \cdot \left(x_0 + \tfrac{h}{2} + z_0 + \tfrac{l_2}{2}\right) = 0.1 \cdot (0.05 + 2 - 0.0525) = 0.1 \cdot 1.9975 = 0.199750 \\
l_3 &= h \cdot \left(x_0 + \tfrac{h}{2} - y_0 - \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right) = 0.1 \cdot (0.05 - 1 - 0.10) = 0.1 \cdot (-1.05) = -0.105 \\
k_4 &= h \cdot \left(x_0 + h + z_0 + l_3\right) = 0.1 \cdot (0.1 + 2 - 0.105) = 0.1 \cdot 1.995 = 0.199500 \\
l_4 &= h \cdot \left(x_0 + h - y_0 - k_3\right) = 0.1 \cdot (0.1 - 1 - 0.199750) = 0.1 \cdot (-1.099750) = -0.109975
\end{aligned}
$$

**Update both $y$ and $z$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= y_0 + \frac{k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{0.200 + 2(0.200) + 2(0.199750) + 0.199500}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{0.200 + 0.400 + 0.399500 + 0.199500}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{1.199000}{6} \\
    &= 1 + 0.199833 \\
    &= 1.199833 \\
\\
z_1 &= z_0 + \frac{l_1 + 2l_2 + 2l_3 + l_4}{6} \\
    &= 2 + \frac{-0.100 + 2(-0.105) + 2(-0.105) + (-0.109975)}{6} \\
    &= 2 + \frac{-0.100 - 0.210 - 0.210 - 0.109975}{6} \\
    &= 2 + \frac{-0.629975}{6} \\
    &= 2 - 0.104996 \\
    &= 1.895004
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** $y(0.1) \approx 1.199833$, $z(0.1) \approx 1.895004$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RK4 Algorithm Flow (Single Step for First Order ODE)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT x0 y0 h"] --> B["STEP 1 Compute k1"]
    B --> B1["k1 = h times f of x0 and y0"]
    B1 --> C["STEP 2 Compute k2"]
    C --> C1["k2 = h times f of x0 plus h by 2 and y0 plus k1 by 2"]
    C1 --> D["STEP 3 Compute k3"]
    D --> D1["k3 = h times f of x0 plus h by 2 and y0 plus k2 by 2"]
    D1 --> E["STEP 4 Compute k4"]
    E --> E1["k4 = h times f of x0 plus h and y0 plus k3"]
    E1 --> F["STEP 5 Compute y1"]
    F --> F1["y1 = y0 plus k1 plus 2k2 plus 2k3 plus k4 all over 6"]
    F1 --> G["OUTPUT y1 at x1 = x0 plus h"]
    G --> H{"More steps needed?"}
    H -->|"Yes"| I["Set x0 = x1, y0 = y1 and repeat"]
    I --> B
    H -->|"No"| J["STOP"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture â€” Higher-Order ODE Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT Second Order ODE y double prime = g of x y and y dash"] --> B["BLOCK 1 Substitute u = y and v = y dash"]
    B --> C["OUTPUT System du by dx = v"]
    B --> D["OUTPUT System dv by dx = g of x u and v"]
    C --> E["BLOCK 2 Apply RK4 in parallel"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Compute k1 k2 k3 k4 for u"]
    E --> G["Compute l1 l2 l3 l4 for v"]
    F --> H["BLOCK 3 Weighted Average"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["OUTPUT u1 and v1 as y1 and y dash of 1"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic â€” Simultaneous ODE Parallel Update

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["INPUT x0 and y10 and y20 and dot dot dot and ym0"]) --> p1["Stage A Compute k1i = h times fi of x0 and all yj0"]
    p1 --> p2["Stage B Compute k2i using x0 plus h by 2 and yj0 plus k1j by 2"]
    p2 --> p3["Stage C Compute k3i using x0 plus h by 2 and yj0 plus k2j by 2"]
    p3 --> p4["Stage D Compute k4i using x0 plus h and yj0 plus k3j"]
    p4 --> p5["Stage E Update yi1 = yi0 plus weighted average"]
    p5 --> out(["OUTPUT y11 and y21 and dot dot dot and ym1"])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each "Stage" in the simultaneous case uses values from **all** variables in the same generation. For example, $k_2$ for $y_1$ uses $l_1$ (from $y_2$) inside its function evaluation. This cross-coupling is the defining feature of simultaneous systems.
