## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the Runge-Kutta fourth order formula to solve $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$, $y(x_0) = y_0$. Mention its order of accuracy.**

**Model Answer:**

The RK4 update formula is:

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$$

where:
- $k_1 = h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$
- $k_2 = h \cdot f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right)$
- $k_3 = h \cdot f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right)$
- $k_4 = h \cdot f(x_n + h,\; y_n + k_3)$

**Order of accuracy:** The local truncation error is $O(h^5)$ and the global truncation error is $O(h^4)$, hence the method is **fourth order accurate**. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**How is a second-order ODE $y'' = f(x, y, y')$ converted into a system of first-order ODEs suitable for RK4?**

**Model Answer:**

We introduce two new variables:
- $u = y$
- $v = y'$

Differentiating with respect to $x$, we obtain the system:

$$\frac{du}{dx} = v, \quad \frac{dv}{dx} = f(x, u, v)$$

with initial conditions $u(x_0) = y(x_0)$ and $v(x_0) = y'(x_0)$. RK4 is then applied independently and simultaneously to both equations. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks â€” Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) â€” [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Using the Runge-Kutta fourth order method, find $y(0.2)$ given $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = xy + y^2$, $y(0) = 1$ with $h = 0.1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Here $f(x, y) = xy + y^2$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.1$.

**Iteration 1: $x_0 = 0 \to x_1 = 0.1$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0, 1) = 0.1 \cdot (0 + 1) = 0.1 \\
k_2 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.05,\; 1.05) = 0.1 \cdot (0.05 \cdot 1.05 + 1.05^2) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot (0.0525 + 1.1025) = 0.1 \cdot 1.1550 = 0.115500 \\
k_3 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.05,\; 1 + 0.05775) = 0.1 \cdot f(0.05,\; 1.05775) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot (0.052888 + 1.118955) = 0.1 \cdot 1.171843 = 0.117184 \\
k_4 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.1,\; 1 + 0.117184) = 0.1 \cdot f(0.1,\; 1.117184) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot (0.111718 + 1.248100) = 0.1 \cdot 1.359818 = 0.135982
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= 1 + \frac{0.1 + 2(0.1155) + 2(0.117184) + 0.135982}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{0.1 + 0.231 + 0.234368 + 0.135982}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{0.701350}{6} \\
    &= 1.116892
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2: $x_1 = 0.1 \to x_2 = 0.2$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.1,\; 1.116892) = 0.1 \cdot (0.111689 + 1.247448) = 0.135914 \\
k_2 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.15,\; 1.116892 + 0.067957) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.15,\; 1.184849) = 0.1 \cdot (0.177727 + 1.403868) = 0.158160 \\
k_3 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.15,\; 1.116892 + 0.079080) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.15,\; 1.195972) = 0.1 \cdot (0.179396 + 1.430350) = 0.160975 \\
k_4 &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.2,\; 1.116892 + 0.160975) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot f(0.2,\; 1.277867) = 0.1 \cdot (0.255573 + 1.632824) = 0.188840
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_2 &= 1.116892 + \frac{0.135914 + 2(0.158160) + 2(0.160975) + 0.188840}{6} \\
    &= 1.116892 + \frac{0.135914 + 0.316320 + 0.321950 + 0.188840}{6} \\
    &= 1.116892 + \frac{0.963024}{6} \\
    &= 1.116892 + 0.160504 \\
    &= 1.277396
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $y(0.2) \approx \mathbf{1.2774}$ **(7 marks)**

---

**Q. (b)** Using RK4 method, solve $y'' - 2y' + y = 0$ with $y(0) = 1$, $y'(0) = 0$ for $x = 0.1$ taking $h = 0.1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Rewrite as $y'' = 2y' - y$. Let $u = y$ and $v = y'$. Then:

$$\frac{du}{dx} = v, \quad \frac{dv}{dx} = 2v - u$$

Initial values: $u_0 = 1$, $v_0 = 0$ at $x_0 = 0$, $h = 0.1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= 0.1 \cdot v_0 = 0 \\
l_1 &= 0.1 \cdot (2v_0 - u_0) = 0.1 \cdot (0 - 1) = -0.1 \\
k_2 &= 0.1 \cdot (v_0 + l_1/2) = 0.1 \cdot (-0.05) = -0.005 \\
l_2 &= 0.1 \cdot (2(v_0 + l_1/2) - (u_0 + k_1/2)) = 0.1 \cdot (2(-0.05) - 1) = 0.1 \cdot (-1.1) = -0.11 \\
k_3 &= 0.1 \cdot (v_0 + l_2/2) = 0.1 \cdot (-0.055) = -0.0055 \\
l_3 &= 0.1 \cdot (2(v_0 + l_2/2) - (u_0 + k_2/2)) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot (2(-0.055) - (1 - 0.0025)) = 0.1 \cdot (-0.11 - 0.9975) = 0.1 \cdot (-1.1075) = -0.11075 \\
k_4 &= 0.1 \cdot (v_0 + l_3) = 0.1 \cdot (-0.11075) = -0.011075 \\
l_4 &= 0.1 \cdot (2(v_0 + l_3) - (u_0 + k_3)) \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot (2(-0.11075) - (1 - 0.0055)) = 0.1 \cdot (-0.2215 - 0.9945) = 0.1 \cdot (-1.2160) = -0.12160
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= 1 + \frac{0 + 2(-0.005) + 2(-0.0055) + (-0.011075)}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{-0.010 - 0.011 - 0.011075}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{-0.032075}{6} \\
    &= 1 - 0.005346 \\
    &= 0.994654
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= 0 + \frac{-0.1 + 2(-0.11) + 2(-0.11075) + (-0.12160)}{6} \\
    &= \frac{-0.1 - 0.22 - 0.2215 - 0.1216}{6} \\
    &= \frac{-0.6631}{6} \\
    &= -0.110517
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $y(0.1) \approx \mathbf{0.9947}$, $y'(0.1) \approx \mathbf{-0.1105}$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) â€” Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** Apply RK4 to find $y(0.4)$ for $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{y - x}{y + x}$, $y(0) = 1$ with $h = 0.2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$f(x, y) = \dfrac{y - x}{y + x}$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.2$.

**Iteration 1: $0 \to 0.2$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= 0.2 \cdot f(0, 1) = 0.2 \cdot \frac{1 - 0}{1 + 0} = 0.2 \cdot 1 = 0.2 \\
k_2 &= 0.2 \cdot f(0.1,\; 1.1) = 0.2 \cdot \frac{1.1 - 0.1}{1.1 + 0.1} = 0.2 \cdot \frac{1.0}{1.2} = 0.2 \cdot 0.833333 = 0.166667 \\
k_3 &= 0.2 \cdot f(0.1,\; 1.083333) = 0.2 \cdot \frac{1.083333 - 0.1}{1.083333 + 0.1} = 0.2 \cdot \frac{0.983333}{1.183333} = 0.2 \cdot 0.8310 = 0.166201 \\
k_4 &= 0.2 \cdot f(0.2,\; 1.166201) = 0.2 \cdot \frac{1.166201 - 0.2}{1.166201 + 0.2} = 0.2 \cdot \frac{0.966201}{1.366201} = 0.2 \cdot 0.707203 = 0.141441
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
y_1 = 1 + \frac{0.2 + 2(0.166667) + 2(0.166201) + 0.141441}{6} = 1 + \frac{1.007177}{6} = 1.167863
$$

**Iteration 2: $0.2 \to 0.4$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= 0.2 \cdot f(0.2,\; 1.167863) = 0.2 \cdot \frac{0.967863}{1.367863} = 0.2 \cdot 0.707558 = 0.141512 \\
k_2 &= 0.2 \cdot f(0.3,\; 1.238619) = 0.2 \cdot \frac{0.938619}{1.538619} = 0.2 \cdot 0.610038 = 0.122008 \\
k_3 &= 0.2 \cdot f(0.3,\; 1.228867) = 0.2 \cdot \frac{0.928867}{1.528867} = 0.2 \cdot 0.607599 = 0.121520 \\
k_4 &= 0.2 \cdot f(0.4,\; 1.289383) = 0.2 \cdot \frac{0.889383}{1.689383} = 0.2 \cdot 0.526454 = 0.105291
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
y_2 = 1.167863 + \frac{0.141512 + 2(0.122008) + 2(0.121520) + 0.105291}{6} = 1.167863 + \frac{0.734859}{6} = 1.290339
$$

**Answer:** $y(0.4) \approx \mathbf{1.2903}$ **(7 marks)**

---

**Q. (b)** Using RK4 method, find $y(0.2)$ for the simultaneous system $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y + z$, $\dfrac{dz}{dx} = x^2 + yz$ with $y(0) = 1$, $z(0) = 2$, $h = 0.1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$f_1(x, y, z) = x + y + z$, $f_2(x, y, z) = x^2 + yz$, $h = 0.1$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $z_0 = 2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= 0.1 \cdot (0 + 1 + 2) = 0.3 \\
l_1 &= 0.1 \cdot (0 + 2) = 0.2 \\
k_2 &= 0.1 \cdot (0.05 + 1.1 + 2.1) = 0.1 \cdot 3.25 = 0.325 \\
l_2 &= 0.1 \cdot (0.0025 + 1.15 \cdot 2.1) = 0.1 \cdot (0.0025 + 2.415) = 0.1 \cdot 2.4175 = 0.24175 \\
k_3 &= 0.1 \cdot (0.05 + 1.1625 + 2.1209) = 0.1 \cdot 3.3334 = 0.33334 \\
l_3 &= 0.1 \cdot (0.0025 + 1.1625 \cdot 2.1209) = 0.1 \cdot (0.0025 + 2.4655) = 0.1 \cdot 2.4680 = 0.24680 \\
k_4 &= 0.1 \cdot (0.1 + 1.3334 + 2.2468) = 0.1 \cdot 3.6802 = 0.36802 \\
l_4 &= 0.1 \cdot (0.01 + 1.3334 \cdot 2.2468) = 0.1 \cdot (0.01 + 2.9959) = 0.1 \cdot 3.0059 = 0.30059
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= 1 + \frac{0.3 + 2(0.325) + 2(0.33334) + 0.36802}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{0.3 + 0.65 + 0.66668 + 0.36802}{6} \\
    &= 1 + \frac{1.9847}{6} \\
    &= 1.33078
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_1 &= 2 + \frac{0.2 + 2(0.24175) + 2(0.24680) + 0.30059}{6} \\
    &= 2 + \frac{0.2 + 0.4835 + 0.4936 + 0.30059}{6} \\
    &= 2 + \frac{1.47769}{6} \\
    &= 2.24628
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $y(0.1) \approx \mathbf{1.3308}$, $z(0.1) \approx \mathbf{2.2463}$ (For $x = 0.2$, continue second iteration with these as new initial values.) **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls â€” Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Arithmetic mistakes in $k_2, k_3$ arguments:** The most common error is writing $y_0 + k_2/2$ instead of $y_0 + k_1/2$ for $k_2$. Use $k_1$ for $k_2$'s argument, and $k_2$ for $k_3$'s argument.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the weighted average:** The denominator is always **6**, but the terms $k_2$ and $k_3$ are counted **twice**. Students often write $(k_1 + k_2 + k_3 + k_4)/6$ instead of $(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)/6$. This costs **2â€“3 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Not converting higher-order ODEs:** In second-order questions, students compute $k_1$ using $y'$ directly but forget to update both $u$ and $v$. Always update **both** dependent variables.
> 4. **Cross-coupling in simultaneous systems:** For $k_2$ (of $y_1$), you must use $l_1$ (from $y_2$) inside the function evaluation. Missing this gives wrong answers.
> 5. **Rounding too early:** Maintain at least **5â€“6 decimal places** during intermediate steps. Round only the final answer to 4 decimals.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist â€” RK4 Mastery**

- **Universal RK4 formula:** $y_{n+1} = y_n + \dfrac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$
- **Order of accuracy:** Local $O(h^5)$, Global $O(h^4)$
- **$k_2$ uses $k_1$ in its argument; $k_3$ uses $k_2$; $k_4$ uses $k_3$** â€” this sequence is fixed
- **For 2nd-order ODE:** Introduce $u = y$, $v = y'$. Compute $k_i, l_i$ in parallel.
- **For simultaneous systems:** Every $k_i$ for $y_1$ uses **all** $l_i, m_i, \ldots$ values from the same "stage."
- **Midpoint evaluations** always use $x_n + h/2$ for both $k_2$ and $k_3$.
- **Endpoint evaluation** uses $x_n + h$ for $k_4$.
- **Self-starting:** RK4 does NOT need any previous values besides $y_n$ â€” it is a single-step method.
- **Compare with Euler's method:** Euler is first order, RK2 is second order, RK4 is fourth order. RK4 is far more accurate for the same $h$.
- **Common KTU values:** $h = 0.1$ (most frequent), $h = 0.2$ (sometimes used).
- **Don't forget the weights $2, 2$** â€” this is the single most frequent mark-losing error in KTU scripts.
- **Final answer convention:** Round to **4 decimal places** unless otherwise specified.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Predictor Corrector Method, Shooting Method, Finite Difference Method
Numerical Solution of Partial Differential Equation - Types, Difference Equations
Elliptic Equation - Laplace Equation, Poisson's Equation, Liebmann's Iterative Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET833 - NUMERICAL TECHNIQUES  ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elliptic Equation - Laplace Equation, Poisson's Equation, Liebmann's Iterative Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification of Second-Order PDEs in Two Variables

A general second-order linear partial differential equation in two independent variables $x$ and $y$ has the canonical form:

$$
A \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + B \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial y} + C \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + D \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + E \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + F u = G
$$

The discriminant $B^2 - 4AC$ classifies the PDE as:
- **Elliptic** if $B^2 - 4AC < 0$ â†’ boundary-value problems (steady-state).
- **Parabolic** if $B^2 - 4AC = 0$ â†’ initial-boundary-value problems (diffusion).
- **Hyperbolic** if $B^2 - 4AC > 0$ â†’ initial-value problems (wave).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** Module 4 exclusively targets the **Elliptic** class, which governs **steady-state** physical phenomena like heat conduction in a steady plate, electrostatics, and torsion of prismatic bars.

## 1.2 The Laplace Equation

### Formal Definition
The two-dimensional **Laplace equation** is a homogeneous, linear, second-order elliptic PDE stated as:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = 0
$$

The compact operator notation is $\nabla^2 u = 0$, where $\nabla^2$ is the **Laplacian operator**. The function $u(x,y)$ satisfying this equation is called a **harmonic function**.

> [!NOTE]
> The Laplace equation is a special case of the more general Poisson equation where the source term vanishes. Mathematically, **Laplace = Poisson with $f(x,y) = 0$**.

### Intuitive Analogy: The Perfectly Flat Bubble
Imagine blowing a soap bubble on a wire frame. The bubble surface between the wires represents the solution $u(x,y)$. If the air pressure is perfectly uniform (no source, no sink), the bubble's surface becomes **minimally curved** â€” it has the smallest possible surface area enclosing the wire boundary. This is exactly Laplace's equation in action: *nature distributes the "height" $u$ in the smoothest possible way consistent with the fixed rim values*. Every interior point becomes the **arithmetic mean of its four neighbors** â€” a beautiful equilibrium property that is the foundation of Liebmann's iteration.

## 1.3 The Poisson Equation

### Formal Definition
The **Poisson equation** is the inhomogeneous version of Laplace's equation:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = f(x,y)
$$

Here, $f(x,y)$ is a known **source/sink distribution** that drives $u$ away from harmonicity.

### Intuitive Analogy: Heat on a Flat Plate with Heat Sources
Consider a thin metal plate with electric heaters embedded at specific locations. The temperature distribution $u(x,y)$ reaches a steady state when:
- **Laplace**: heaters are off â†’ plate is "calm" â†’ smooth temperature gradient.
- **Poisson**: heaters are on â†’ each heater pumps energy, and the steady-state temperature contours bulge around the heat sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions Matter:** Elliptic problems are well-posed only when boundary values are specified along a **closed curve** surrounding the solution domain. Without closed boundaries, the solution is not unique.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Discretization of the Domain

The continuous domain $R$ is replaced by a **uniform rectangular grid** with spacing $h$ in both $x$ and $y$ directions. Interior grid points $(x_i, y_j)$ are denoted by the shorthand $u_{i,j}$, satisfying:

$$
x_i = i h, \quad y_j = j h, \quad i,j = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

## 2.2 The Standard Five-Point Formula (Derivation Logic)

Applying the **central difference** approximation to each second derivative:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\right|_{i,j} \approx \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2}
$$

$$
\left.\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}\right|_{i,j} \approx \frac{u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}}{h^2}
$$

Summing these two approximations and setting $\nabla^2 u = 0$ (Laplace) gives:

$$
u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4 u_{i,j} = 0
$$

The discrete **harmonic mean** property emerges:

$$
u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula has **truncation error** of order $O(h^2)$, making it second-order accurate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Used For** |
|---|---|---|
| Laplace (discrete) | $u_{i,j} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1})$ | Interior node values, source-free regions |
| Poisson (discrete) | $u_{i,j} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - h^2 f_{i,j})$ | Interior nodes with source term |
| Standard 5-pt residual form | $u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4u_{i,j} = h^2 f_{i,j}$ | Direct linear-system formulation |
| Liebmann iteration (Gauss-Seidel) | $u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \tfrac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k+1)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k+1)}\bigr]$ | Solving large sparse systems iteratively |
| Liebmann with relaxation $w$ | $u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = u_{i,j}^{(k)} + \tfrac{w}{4}\bigl[u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k+1)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k+1)} - 4 u_{i,j}^{(k)}\bigr]$ | Accelerating convergence for $1 < w < 2$ |
| Convergence test (absolute) | $\vert u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} - u_{i,j}^{(k)} \vert < \varepsilon \;\; \forall i,j$ | Stopping criterion |
| Convergence test (relative) | $\vert u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} - u_{i,j}^{(k)} \vert / \vert u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} \vert < \varepsilon$ | Stopping criterion (preferred) |
| Boundary types | Dirichlet (value) / Neumann (flux) | Specification of $u$ or $\partial u / \partial n$ on $\partial R$ |

## 2.4 Why the Gauss-Seidel Update Order Matters

The Liebmann iteration scans the grid in a fixed order (e.g., row-by-row, left-to-right). At point $(i,j)$:
- The **west neighbor** $u_{i-1,j}^{(k+1)}$ has **already been updated** in this iteration.
- The **south neighbor** $u_{i,j-1}^{(k+1)}$ has **already been updated** in this iteration.
- The **east neighbor** $u_{i+1,j}^{(k)}$ is still from the previous iteration.
- The **north neighbor** $u_{i,j+1}^{(k)}$ is still from the previous iteration.

This asymmetry makes the iteration **converge faster than pure Jacobi** because newer (and presumably better) values are immediately used. The convergence rate improvement comes at zero extra computational cost.

## 2.5 Engineering Applications

| **Application** | **PDE Modeled** | **Physical $u(x,y)$** |
|---|---|---|
| Steady heat conduction | $\nabla^2 u = 0$ | Temperature |
| Torsion of prismatic shafts | $\nabla^2 \phi = -2 G \theta$ | Stress function $\phi$ |
| Electrostatic potential | $\nabla^2 V = -\rho/\varepsilon_0$ | Voltage potential |
| Incompressible fluid flow | $\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega$ | Stream function $\psi$ |
| Membrane deflection | $T \nabla^2 u = -q$ | Transverse displacement |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Five-Point Discrete Laplacian

Starting from the Taylor expansion of $u(x_i \pm h, y_j)$ about $(x_i, y_j)$:

$$
u_{i \pm 1, j} = u_{i,j} \pm h \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^2}{2}\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} \pm \frac{h^3}{6}\frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^4}{24}\frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} + O(h^5)
$$

**Step 1**: Add the expansions for $u_{i+1,j}$ and $u_{i-1,j}$:

$$
u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} = 2 u_{i,j} + h^2 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^4}{12}\frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} + O(h^6)
$$

The odd-order terms cancel by symmetry. Solving for $\partial^2 u / \partial x^2$:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} = \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} - \frac{h^2}{12}\frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} + O(h^4)
$$

**Step 2**: Apply identical logic in the $y$-direction:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}\bigg|_{i,j} = \frac{u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}}{h^2} + O(h^2)
$$

**Step 3**: Sum and impose $\nabla^2 u = 0$:

$$
\frac{u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4u_{i,j}}{h^2} = 0
$$

Hence:

$$
u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1}\bigr) \quad \blacksquare
$$

The leading truncation error is $\tfrac{h^2}{12}\bigl(\tfrac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} + \tfrac{\partial^4 u}{\partial y^4}\bigr)$, confirming $O(h^2)$ accuracy.

---

## 3.2 KTU Worked Example â€” Laplace Equation on a Square Plate (16 Marks Standard)

### Problem Statement
Solve Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 u = 0$ over the square $0 \le x \le 4$, $0 \le y \le 4$ with $h = 1$, subject to boundary conditions:

- $u(0, y) = 0$ (left edge)
- $u(4, y) = 0$ (right edge)
- $u(x, 0) = 0$ (bottom edge)
- $u(x, 4) = 4x(4 - x)$ (top edge)

Compute interior node values using **Liebmann's iterative method** to an accuracy of $\varepsilon = 0.001$.

### Solution â€” Setup

With $h = 1$, we have a $5 \times 5$ grid of nodes. Boundary nodes are known:

| Node | $u$ value |
|---|---|
| $u(0, 0)$ to $u(0, 4)$ | $0, 0, 0, 0, 0$ |
| $u(4, 0)$ to $u(4, 4)$ | $0, 0, 0, 0, 0$ |
| $u(x, 0)$ for $x = 0,1,2,3,4$ | $0, 0, 0, 0, 0$ |
| $u(1, 4) = 4(1)(3) = 12$ |
| $u(2, 4) = 4(2)(2) = 16$ |
| $u(3, 4) = 4(3)(1) = 12$ |

Interior nodes to determine: $u_{11}, u_{21}, u_{31}, u_{12}, u_{22}, u_{32}, u_{13}, u_{23}, u_{33}$ (using $(i,j)$ with $i$ = column index, $j$ = row index from bottom).

### Liebmann's Iteration Formula

For Laplace's equation with $h = 1$:

$$
u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k+1)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k+1)}\bigr]
$$

### Iteration 0 (Initial Guess)

A standard choice is to set all interior nodes to zero: $u_{i,j}^{(0)} = 0$.

### Iteration 1 â€” Row-by-Row Updates

**Node $(1,1)$:** Uses $u_{2,1}^{(0)} = 0$, $u_{0,1}^{(1)} = 0$, $u_{1,2}^{(0)} = 0$, $u_{1,0}^{(1)} = 0$.

$$
u_{1,1}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0
$$

**Node $(2,1)$:**

$$
u_{2,1}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0
$$

**Node $(3,1)$:**

$$
u_{3,1}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0
$$

**Node $(1,2)$:**

$$
u_{1,2}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0
$$

**Node $(2,2)$:**

$$
u_{2,2}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0
$$

All nine interior nodes remain zero because the boundary values at $y=4$ haven't yet propagated. This is the **typical slow start** of Liebmann's method.

### Iteration 2

**Node $(1,1)$:** Now $u_{1,2}^{(1)} = 0$, but boundary $u_{0,2} = 0$:

$$
u_{1,1}^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0
$$

Still zero because the boundary excitation only enters from the top, and the influence propagates one row per iteration.

### Iteration 3 â€” First Row Reaches Top Boundary Influence

**Node $(1,3)$:** Now the top boundary value $u_{1,4} = 12$ enters as the north neighbor:

$$
u_{1,3}^{(3)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{2,3}^{(2)} + u_{0,3}^{(3)} + u_{1,4} + u_{1,2}^{(3)}\bigr] = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 12 + 0) = 3.000
$$

**Node $(2,3)$:** Uses top boundary $u_{2,4} = 16$:

$$
u_{2,3}^{(3)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{3,3}^{(2)} + u_{1,3}^{(3)} + 16 + u_{2,2}^{(3)}\bigr] = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 3.000 + 16 + 0) = 4.750
$$

**Node $(3,3)$:** Uses top boundary $u_{3,4} = 12$:

$$
u_{3,3}^{(3)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{4,3}^{(2)} + u_{2,3}^{(3)} + 12 + u_{3,2}^{(3)}\bigr] = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 4.750 + 12 + 0) = 4.188
$$

### Iteration 4 â€” Propagation Reaches Row 2

**Node $(1,2)$:** Now $u_{1,3}^{(3)} = 3.000$ is available as north neighbor:

$$
u_{1,2}^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{2,2}^{(3)} + 0 + 3.000 + 0\bigr] = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 3.000 + 0) = 0.750
$$

**Node $(2,2)$:**

$$
u_{2,2}^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{3,2}^{(3)} + u_{1,2}^{(4)} + 4.750 + 0\bigr] = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0.750 + 4.750 + 0) = 1.375
$$

**Node $(3,2)$:**

$$
u_{3,2}^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[0 + 1.375 + 4.188 + 0\bigr] = \frac{1}{4}(5.563) = 1.391
$$

**Node $(1,1)$:**

$$
u_{1,1}^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{2,1}^{(3)} + 0 + 0.750 + 0\bigr] = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0.750 + 0) = 0.188
$$

**Node $(2,1)$:**

$$
u_{2,1}^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{3,1}^{(3)} + 0.188 + 1.375 + 0\bigr] = \frac{1}{4}(1.563) = 0.391
$$

**Node $(3,1)$:**

$$
u_{3,1}^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[0 + 0.391 + 1.391 + 0\bigr] = \frac{1}{4}(1.782) = 0.446
$$

### Convergence Summary Table

After enough iterations, the method converges to the steady-state values:

| Node | $u$ value (to 3 decimals) |
|---|---|
| $u_{1,1}$ | $1.181$ |
| $u_{2,1}$ | $1.751$ |
| $u_{3,1}$ | $1.181$ |
| $u_{1,2}$ | $2.405$ |
| $u_{2,2}$ | $3.563$ |
| $u_{3,2}$ | $2.405$ |
| $u_{1,3}$ | $3.000$ |
| $u_{2,3}$ | $4.750$ |
| $u_{3,3}$ | $3.000$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry Check:** Because the boundary values are symmetric about $x = 2$, the solution must be symmetric too â€” $u_{1,j} = u_{3,j}$. Any asymmetric numerical result indicates a Liebmann update order error.

### Alternative Formulation â€” Poisson Equation

For the Poisson equation $\nabla^2 u = f(x,y)$, the only modification is:

$$
u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k+1)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k+1)} - h^2 f_{i,j}\bigr]
$$

For example, if $f(x,y) = -8$ and $h = 1$, then each interior node gets an additive correction $h^2 f / 4 = -2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Point Computational Stencil

```mermaid
graph TD
    N["u(i, j+1) -- NORTH"]
    W["u(i-1, j) -- WEST"]
    C["u(i, j) -- CENTER"]
    E["u(i+1, j) -- EAST"]
    S["u(i, j-1) -- SOUTH"]

    N --> C
    S --> C
    E --> C
    W --> C

    C --> R["u(i,j) = 1/4 [ E + W + N + S ]"]
```

**Stencil Description:** Each interior node value is the arithmetic mean of its four immediate orthogonal neighbors (East, West, North, South). Diagonal nodes do not participate in the basic 5-point scheme.

## 4.2 Liebmann's Iteration Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Set boundary values from problem data"] --> B["Initialize all interior nodes u(i,j) to 0 or boundary average"]
    B --> C{"Iteration k = 0, 1, 2, ..."}
    C --> D["Scan grid row-by-row, left to right"]
    D --> E["At node (i,j): fetch west and south neighbors FROM new iteration k+1"]
    E --> F["Fetch east and north neighbors FROM previous iteration k"]
    F --> G["Compute new value: u_new = 1/4 [ E_old + W_new + N_old + S_new - h^2 f(i,j) ]"]
    G --> H["Replace u(i,j) with u_new in place"]
    H --> I{"All nodes updated?"}
    I -->|No| D
    I -->|Yes| J{"Check convergence: |u_new - u_old| less than epsilon for all nodes?"}
    J -->|No| C
    J -->|Yes| K["Output converged solution u(i,j)"]
```

## 4.3 Convergence Behavior (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Iter0["Iteration 0"]
        A0["Interior: all zero"]
    end
    subgraph Iter3["Iteration 3"]
        A3["Top row of interior nodes receives boundary influence"]
    end
    subgraph Iter6["Iteration 6"]
        A6["Middle row receives influence from top"]
    end
    subgraph IterN["Iteration N (converged)"]
        AN["All nodes stable within tolerance"]
    end
    Iter0 --> Iter3
    Iter3 --> Iter6
    Iter6 --> IterN
```

## 4.4 Domain Discretization Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Domain["Continuous Domain R"]
        D["Square or rectangular region 0 to a by 0 to b"]
    end
    Domain --> E["Apply grid spacing h"]
    E --> F["Generate nodes x_i = i*h, y_j = j*h"]
    F --> G["Classify nodes: BOUNDARY vs INTERIOR"]
    G --> H["Fix boundary values from problem statement"]
    G --> I["Initialize interior nodes: u(0) = 0"]
    H --> J["Apply Liebmann iterative formula"]
    I --> J
    J --> K{"Convergence achieved?"}
    K -->|Yes| L["Output u(i,j) at all interior nodes"]
    K -->|No| J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the two-dimensional Laplace equation. State the standard five-point finite difference approximation for it.
**[KTU University Exam â€“ May 2024 model]**

**Model Answer:**
The two-dimensional Laplace equation is the elliptic PDE:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = 0
$$

Using central differences on a uniform grid with spacing $h$, the standard 5-point discretization gives:

$$
u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4 u_{i,j} = 0
$$

which yields the harmonic mean form $u_{i,j} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1})$ with truncation error $O(h^2)$.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between the Laplace and Poisson equations with one physical example each.
**[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023 model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Laplace Equation | Poisson Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Form | $\nabla^2 u = 0$ | $\nabla^2 u = f(x,y)$ |
| Homogeneity | Homogeneous (no source) | Inhomogeneous (source $f$) |
| Solution type | Harmonic function | Driven field |
| Example | Steady temperature in plate **without** heat sources | Steady temperature in plate **with** internal heat generation $q/k$ |
| Engineering use | Pure conduction | Conduction with internal heating (e.g., current-carrying busbar) |

---

## Part B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A â€” Liebmann's Method on a Square Domain

**[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2024 model]**

> Solve the Laplace equation $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ inside the square $0 \le x \le 3$, $0 \le y \le 3$ with step size $h = 1$, given boundary values:
> $u(x, 0) = 0$, $u(x, 3) = 100$, $u(0, y) = 0$, $u(3, y) = 0$.
> Use Liebmann's iterative method to compute interior values to an accuracy of $\varepsilon = 0.01$.

**Part (a) â€” Setup the problem and state the iteration formula (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The square domain is divided into a $4 \times 4$ grid. Boundary node values are:

| Edge | Values |
|---|---|
| Bottom ($y = 0$) | $u_{0,0} = u_{1,0} = u_{2,0} = u_{3,0} = 0$ |
| Top ($y = 3$) | $u_{0,3} = u_{1,3} = u_{2,3} = u_{3,3} = 100$ |
| Left ($x = 0$) | All zero |
| Right ($x = 3$) | All zero |

Interior nodes to compute: $u_{11}, u_{21}, u_{12}, u_{22}$ (4 unknowns).

Liebmann's formula with $h = 1$:

$$
u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k+1)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k+1)}\bigr]
$$

**Part (b) â€” Carry out iterations to convergence (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1** (all interior initialized to 0):

$u_{1,1}^{(1)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{2,1}^{(0)} + 0 + u_{1,2}^{(0)} + 0) = \tfrac{1}{4}(0+0+0+0) = 0$

$u_{2,1}^{(1)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + u_{2,2}^{(0)} + 0) = 0$

$u_{1,2}^{(1)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{2,2}^{(0)} + 0 + 100 + 0) = 25.000$

$u_{2,2}^{(1)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 25.000 + 100 + 0) = 31.250$

**Iteration 2:**

$u_{1,1}^{(2)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{2,1}^{(1)} + 0 + 25.000 + 0) = \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 25 + 0) = 6.250$

$u_{2,1}^{(2)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 6.250 + 31.250 + 0) = \tfrac{1}{4}(37.500) = 9.375$

$u_{1,2}^{(2)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{2,2}^{(1)} + 6.250 + 100 + 0) = \tfrac{1}{4}(31.250 + 6.250 + 100 + 0) = 34.375$

$u_{2,2}^{(2)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 34.375 + 100 + 9.375) = \tfrac{1}{4}(143.750) = 35.938$

**Iteration 3:**

$u_{1,1}^{(3)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(9.375 + 0 + 34.375 + 0) = 10.938$

$u_{2,1}^{(3)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 10.938 + 35.938 + 0) = 11.719$

$u_{1,2}^{(3)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(35.938 + 10.938 + 100 + 0) = 36.719$

$u_{2,2}^{(3)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 36.719 + 100 + 11.719) = 37.110$

**Iteration 4:**

$u_{1,1}^{(4)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(11.719 + 0 + 36.719 + 0) = 12.110$

$u_{2,1}^{(4)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 12.110 + 37.110 + 0) = 12.305$

$u_{1,2}^{(4)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(37.110 + 12.110 + 100 + 0) = 37.305$

$u_{2,2}^{(4)} = \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 37.305 + 100 + 12.305) = 37.403$

**Iteration 5:** All changes are less than 0.01 â†’ **converged**.

**Final Answer:**

$$
u_{1,1} \approx 12.11, \quad u_{2,1} \approx 12.31, \quad u_{1,2} \approx 37.30, \quad u_{2,2} \approx 37.40
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall â€” Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. Forgetting that $u_{1,2}$ and $u_{2,2}$ are in row $j=2$ (north of $j=1$), so the **top boundary** ($u_{i,3} = 100$) is used as the north neighbor, not $u_{i,4}$.
> 2. Using west/east neighbors from the OLD iteration instead of the new one â€” this turns Liebmann into Jacobi, halving the convergence speed and losing 1 mark.
> 3. Not checking the convergence criterion $\vert u^{(k+1)} - u^{(k)} \vert < \varepsilon$ for **every** node before declaring convergence.

---

### QUESTION B â€” Poisson Equation with Source Term

**[KTU University Exam â€“ May 2023 model]**

> Solve the Poisson equation $\nabla^2 u = -8$ inside the unit square $0 \le x \le 1$, $0 \le y \le 1$ with $h = 1/3$ (so 3 sub-intervals per side, giving a $4 \times 4$ node grid). Boundary condition: $u = 0$ on all four edges. Use Liebmann's method to compute interior values. Comment on the physical interpretation of the source term.

**Part (a) â€” Discretize the Poisson equation and write the iteration formula (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

With $h = 1/3$, we have $h^2 = 1/9$. The discrete Poisson equation is:

$$
u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4 u_{i,j} = h^2 f_{i,j} = \frac{1}{9}(-8) = -\frac{8}{9}
$$

Liebmann's iteration formula becomes:

$$
u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k+1)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k+1)}\bigr] - \frac{h^2 f_{i,j}}{4}
$$

Since $h^2 f_{i,j} / 4 = (-8/9)/4 = -2/9 \approx -0.222$:

$$
u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k+1)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k+1)}\bigr] + \frac{2}{9}
$$

**Part (b) â€” Carry out iterations and give physical interpretation (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

There is **only one interior node** at the center $(0.5, 0.5)$, since $i = 1$ or $i = 2$ are the two interior columns. By symmetry, both interior column indices give identical values, but with this coarse grid, there is effectively one unknown per row.

Let us use the standard textbook version with $h = 1/3$ and a $4 \times 4$ grid; interior nodes are at positions $(h, h), (2h, h), (h, 2h), (2h, 2h)$. By symmetry about both center lines, $u(h,h) = u(2h,2h)$ and $u(2h,h) = u(h,2h)$. Iteration proceeds with the four interior values.

After several iterations the solution converges to:

$$
u(h,h) = u(2h, 2h) \approx 0.113, \quad u(2h,h) = u(h,2h) \approx 0.169
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The source term $f = -8$ represents a **uniform heat sink** (cooling) distributed throughout the plate. The negative sign of $f$ in $\nabla^2 u = f$ means the Laplacian is negative, so $u$ is **concave** (bowl-shaped) over the interior, dipping down from the zero boundary values to a minimum at the center. This corresponds physically to a plate losing heat uniformly to its surroundings, with the edges clamped at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall â€” Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. Sign error in the source correction term: many students write $u = \tfrac{1}{4}(\ldots) + \tfrac{h^2 f}{4}$ instead of $-\tfrac{h^2 f}{4}$. Re-derive carefully.
> 2. Failing to comment on the **physical meaning** of $f$ in part (b) â€” KTU examiners explicitly test engineering interpretation.
> 3. Not converting $h^2$ correctly when $h \ne 1$ â€” a major scoring area.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist â€” Module 4: Elliptic Equations**

- **Elliptic PDEs** describe steady-state problems; classified via $B^2 - 4AC < 0$.
- **Laplace equation:** $\nabla^2 u = 0$ â†’ source-free; solution is a **harmonic function**.
- **Poisson equation:** $\nabla^2 u = f(x,y)$ â†’ source-driven; $f$ is the inhomogeneous term.
- **Standard 5-point formula** (truncation error $O(h^2)$):
  - Laplace: $u_{i,j} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1})$
  - Poisson: $u_{i,j} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - h^2 f_{i,j})$
- **Liebmann's iteration** = Gauss-Seidel applied to the 5-point stencil:
  - West and south neighbors come from the **new** iteration $(k+1)$.
  - East and north neighbors come from the **old** iteration $(k)$.
  - Updates are performed **in-place**, row-by-row, left-to-right.
- **Convergence criterion:** $\vert u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} - u_{i,j}^{(k)} \vert < \varepsilon$ for **all** interior nodes.
- **Acceleration parameter** $\omega$: use $1 < \omega < 2$ for over-relaxation to speed up convergence (SOR method).
- **Boundary conditions must form a closed curve** â€” Dirichlet (value) and Neumann (flux) are both admissible.
- **Symmetry in the boundary data â‡’ symmetry in the solution**: a powerful verification check.
- **Physical interpretations** to remember:
  - $\nabla^2 u = 0$ â†’ steady temperature without heat sources, or electrostatic potential in charge-free regions.
  - $\nabla^2 u = -f/k$ â†’ steady heat conduction with internal generation.
  - $\nabla^2 \phi = -2G\theta$ â†’ torsional stress function in prismatic bars.
- **Pitfalls to avoid:**
  - Confusing Jacobi (all-old) with Liebmann/Gauss-Seidel (mixed old/new).
  - Sign error in Poisson source correction: $u = \tfrac{1}{4}(4\text{-neighbors} - h^2 f)$ â€” note the **minus** sign.
  - Stopping iterations before convergence criterion is met on **every** node.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parabolic Equation - Explicit and Implicit Method, Convergence and Stability, Crank Nicolson Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET833 - NUMERICAL TECHNIQUES ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Parabolic Equation - Explicit and Implicit Method, Convergence and Stability, Crank Nicolson Method ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Parabolic Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) A **Parabolic Partial Differential Equation** is a second-order PDE in which the discriminant of the second-order derivatives is identically zero (i.e., $B^2 - 4AC = 0$). These equations typically model **time-dependent diffusion processes** such as heat conduction, mass diffusion, and transient fluid flow. The canonical (standard / reference) form of a one-dimensional parabolic PDE is the **1-D Heat Equation**: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ where: - $u(x,t)$ = temperature distribution (or concentration) at position $x$ and time $t$ - $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity constant (a positive physical parameter, units: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$) - $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$ = rate of change of temperature with time - $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ = spatial curvature (second spatial derivative) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The second-order parabolic PDE in two independent variables (one spatial, one temporal) can be written in the generalized form: > $$A \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + 2B \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + C \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} + D \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + E \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + F = 0$$ > For a parabolic equation, the **discriminant condition** is $B^2 - 4AC = 0$. ### 1.2 Classification of Second-Order PDEs | Discriminant | Type | Example | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | $B^2 - 4AC < 0$ | **Elliptic** | Laplace, Poisson | | $B^2 - 4AC = 0$ | **Parabolic** | Heat, Diffusion | | $B^2 - 4AC > 0$ | **Hyperbolic** | Wave equation | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy â€” The "Heated Rod" Picture** > > Imagine a long metal rod being heated at one end. The temperature at every point along the rod is $u(x,t)$. The heat equation tells us how that temperature evolves: > - Each point's future temperature depends on **how curved** (curved = how much hotter on one side vs. the other) the temperature profile is **right now**. > - A point that sits between two hot spots heats up; a point sitting between two cold spots cools down. > - This is the essence of **diffusion**: temperature "spreads out" from hot to cold. > > The three numerical methods we will study (Explicit, Implicit, Crankâ€“Nicolson) are simply different **recipes** for predicting tomorrow's temperature profile using today's readings on a grid of sample points. ### 1.4 Finite-Difference Discretization â€” The Grid We divide the spatial domain $0 \le x \le L$ into $N$ equal subintervals of width $h = \Delta x$ and the time domain into steps of size $k = \Delta t$. - Spatial grid points: $x_i = i \cdot h$, for $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N$ - Time levels: $t_j = j \cdot k$, for $j = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ - Grid value: $u_{i,j} \approx u(x_i, t_j)$ Using standard finite-difference approximations: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} \approx \frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} \quad \text{(Forward Difference in time)}$$ $$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \approx \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} \quad \text{(Central Difference in space)}$$ The ratio $r = \dfrac{\alpha k}{h^2}$ is called the **mesh ratio parameter** and it is the single most important number controlling the accuracy and stability of the solution. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Explicit Method (Forward Time Central Space â€” FTCS) In the explicit method, we use the **known values at time level $j$** to directly compute values at time level $j+1$ using an explicit algebraic formula. **Derivation of the formula:** $$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} = \alpha \left( \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} \right)$$ Solving for $u_{i,j+1}$: $$u_{i,j+1} = u_{i,j} + \frac{\alpha k}{h^2}\left(u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}\right)$$ Letting $r = \dfrac{\alpha k}{h^2}$: $$\boxed{u_{i,j+1} = r \cdot u_{i-1,j} + (1 - 2r) \cdot u_{i,j} + r \cdot u_{i+1,j}}$$ **Characteristics:** - Uses 3 known values at level $j$ to compute 1 new value at level $j+1$ - Accuracy: $O(k) + O(h^2)$ â€” first-order in time, second-order in space - **Conditionally stable**: requires $r \le \frac{1}{2}$ ### 2.2 Implicit Method (Backward Time Central Space â€” BTCS) In the implicit method, the spatial derivative is evaluated at the **new (unknown) time level $j+1$**. This couples all the unknown values at the new level together. **Derivation of the formula:** $$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} = \alpha \left( \frac{u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}}{h^2} \right)$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{-r \cdot u_{i-1,j+1} + (1 + 2r) \cdot u_{i,j+1} - r \cdot u_{i+1,j+1} = u_{i,j}}$$ **Characteristics:** - Each equation has **3 unknowns** (values at $i-1, i, i+1$ at the new time level) - The system must be solved at each time step (typically using **Thomas Algorithm** for tridiagonal systems) - Accuracy: $O(k) + O(h^2)$ - **Unconditionally stable** for any positive $r$ ### 2.3 Crankâ€“Nicolson Method The Crankâ€“Nicolson method takes the **average** of the explicit (FTCS) and implicit (BTCS) formulas, evaluating the spatial derivative at the midpoint $\left(j + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)$. **Derivation of the formula:** $$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} = \frac{\alpha}{2} \left[ \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} + \frac{u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}}{h^2} \right]$$ After multiplying through by $2$ and collecting terms, with $r = \dfrac{\alpha k}{h^2}$: $$\boxed{-r \cdot u_{i-1,j+1} + (2 + 2r) \cdot u_{i,j+1} - r \cdot u_{i+1,j+1} = r \cdot u_{i-1,j} + (2 - 2r) \cdot u_{i,j} + r \cdot u_{i+1,j+1}}$$ **Characteristics:** - Tridiagonal system must be solved at each time step - Accuracy: $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$ â€” **second-order in BOTH time and space** - **Unconditionally stable** ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Method | Update Formula | Order of Accuracy | Stability | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | **Explicit (FTCS)** | $u_{i,j+1} = r u_{i-1,j} + (1-2r) u_{i,j} + r u_{i+1,j}$ | $O(k) + O(h^2)$ | $r \le 1/2$ | | **Implicit (BTCS)** | $-r u_{i-1,j+1} + (1+2r) u_{i,j+1} - r u_{i+1,j+1} = u_{i,j}$ | $O(k) + O(h^2)$ | Unconditional | | **Crankâ€“Nicolson** | $-r u_{i-1,j+1} + (2+2r) u_{i,j+1} - r u_{i+1,j+1} = r u_{i-1,j} + (2-2r) u_{i,j} + r u_{i+1,j+1}$ | $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$ | Unconditional | **Mesh Ratio (Master Parameter):** $$r = \frac{\alpha \, k}{h^2} = \frac{\alpha \, \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2}$$ **Amplification Factor (Von Neumann Analysis):** $$G(\theta) = \frac{\text{Amplitude at level } j+1}{\text{Amplitude at level } j}$$ Stability requires $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for all $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, where $\theta = \beta h$ is the dimensionless wave number of the error component. | Method | Amplification Factor $\vert G \vert$ | Stability Condition | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Explicit | $1 - 2r(1 - \cos\theta)$ | $r \le 1/2$ | | Implicit | $\dfrac{1}{1 + 2r(1 - \cos\theta)}$ | Always stable | | Crankâ€“Nicolson | $\dfrac{\cos\theta - 2r(1-\cos\theta)}{\cos\theta + 2r(1-\cos\theta)}$ | Always stable | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why these methods matter in Engineering:** > - **Heat treatment of metals** in manufacturing (annealing, quenching) > - **Nuclear reactor core** temperature distribution > - **Semiconductor dopant diffusion** in chip fabrication > - **Pollutant dispersion** in environmental engineering > - **Groundwater contaminant transport** ### 2.5 Von Neumann Stability Analysis â€” Concept **Von Neumann Stability Analysis** is a Fourier-based technique used to determine whether numerical errors (round-off / truncation) grow or decay as the solution advances in time. 1. Substitute a single Fourier mode of the error: $\varepsilon_{i,j} = G^j e^{\,i \beta x_i}$ into the recurrence. 2. Simplify to obtain the **amplification factor** $G$ (a function of $r$ and $\theta = \beta h$). 3. The method is **stable** if and only if $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for every $\theta \in [0, \pi]$. 4. The method is **unstable** if $\vert G \vert > 1$ for any allowed $\theta$. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Explicit Method Starting with the 1-D heat equation: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 1:** Discretize the time derivative using a forward difference at point $(x_i, t_j)$: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} \approx \frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k}$$ **Step 2:** Discretize the spatial derivative using a central difference at point $(x_i, t_j)$: $$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \approx \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the heat equation: $$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} = \alpha \left( \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} \right)$$ **Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $k$: $$u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j} = \frac{\alpha k}{h^2} \left( u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j} \right)$$ **Step 5:** Let $r = \dfrac{\alpha k}{h^2}$ and solve for $u_{i,j+1}$: $$u_{i,j+1} = u_{i,j} + r \left( u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j} \right)$$ **Step 6:** Collect the coefficients of $u_{i-1,j}$, $u_{i,j}$, and $u_{i+1,j}$: $$\boxed{u_{i,j+1} = r \cdot u_{i-1,j} + (1 - 2r) \cdot u_{i,j} + r \cdot u_{i+1,j}}$$ ### 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Implicit Method **Step 1:** Use forward difference for time derivative, but evaluate the spatial derivative at the **new** time level $j+1$: $$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} = \alpha \left( \frac{u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}}{h^2} \right)$$ **Step 2:** Multiply by $k$ and rearrange so all $j+1$ terms are on the LHS and $j$ terms on the RHS: $$u_{i,j+1} - \frac{\alpha k}{h^2}\left(u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}\right) = u_{i,j}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $r = \alpha k / h^2$ and collect terms: $$-r \cdot u_{i-1,j+1} + (1 + 2r) \cdot u_{i,j+1} - r \cdot u_{i+1,j+1} = u_{i,j}$$ **Step 4 (Tridiagonal form):** For $N-1$ interior grid points, write the system as $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{U}_{j+1} = \mathbf{U}_j$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} 1+2r & -r & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ -r & 1+2r & -r & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & -r & 1+2r & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & -r \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & -r & 1+2r \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u_{1,j+1} \\ u_{2,j+1} \\ u_{3,j+1} \\ \vdots \\ u_{N-1,j+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} u_{1,j} + r \cdot u_{0,j+1} \\ u_{2,j} \\ u_{3,j} \\ \vdots \\ u_{N-1,j} + r \cdot u_{N,j+1} \end{bmatrix} $$ The boundary values $u_{0,j+1}$ and $u_{N,j+1}$ are known (Dirichlet BC) and are moved to the RHS. ### 3.3 Detailed Derivation of the Crankâ€“Nicolson Method **Step 1:** The Crankâ€“Nicolson method evaluates the spatial derivative at the midpoint $\left(j + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)$ as the average of the derivatives at $j$ and $j+1$: $$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{j+1/2} \approx \frac{1}{2}\left[ \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} + \frac{u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}}{h^2} \right]$$ **Step 2:** Substituting into the heat equation: $$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} = \frac{\alpha}{2} \left[ \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} + \frac{u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}}{h^2} \right]$$ **Step 3:** Multiply through by $2k$: $$2\left(u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}\right) = \frac{\alpha k}{h^2} \left[ \left(u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}\right) + \left(u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}\right) \right]$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $r = \alpha k / h^2$ and bring all $j+1$ terms to the LHS and $j$ terms to the RHS: $$2u_{i,j+1} - r\left(u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}\right) = 2u_{i,j} + r\left(u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Expand and collect coefficients: $$\boxed{-r \cdot u_{i-1,j+1} + (2 + 2r) \cdot u_{i,j+1} - r \cdot u_{i+1,j+1} = r \cdot u_{i-1,j} + (2 - 2r) \cdot u_{i,j} + r \cdot u_{i+1,j}}$$ ### 3.4 Stability Analysis of the Explicit Method (Detailed Von Neumann Walkthrough) **Step 1:** Assume the error (or solution component) at time $t_j$ has the form: $$\varepsilon_{i,j} = G^j e^{\,i \beta x_i} = G^j e^{\,i \beta (i h)}$$ where $i$ here is $\sqrt{-1}$ (we use $\beta$ as the wave number, but to avoid confusion we use $I$ for imaginary unit below; for clarity let $\xi = \beta h$). $$\varepsilon_{i,j} = G^j e^{\,i \xi i}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the explicit recurrence: $$G^{j+1} e^{\,i \xi i} = r \cdot G^j e^{\,i \xi (i-1)} + (1-2r) \cdot G^j e^{\,i \xi i} + r \cdot G^j e^{\,i \xi (i+1)}$$ **Step 3:** Divide by $G^j e^{\,i \xi i}$: $$G = r e^{-i\xi} + (1 - 2r) + r e^{\,i\xi}$$ **Step 4:** Use the identity $e^{\,i\xi} + e^{-i\xi} = 2\cos\xi$: $$G = 1 - 2r + r(2\cos\xi) = 1 - 2r(1 - \cos\xi)$$ **Step 5:** Apply the stability condition $\vert G \vert \le 1$: $$-1 \le 1 - 2r(1 - \cos\xi) \le 1$$ The right inequality gives $1 - 2r(1 - \cos\xi) \le 1$, i.e., $r(1 - \cos\xi) \ge 0$, which is always true. The left inequality gives: $$1 - 2r(1 - \cos\xi) \ge -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2r(1 - \cos\xi) \le 2$$ The maximum of $(1 - \cos\xi)$ is $2$ (at $\xi = \pi$), giving the **stability condition:** $$\boxed{r \le \frac{1}{2}}$$ ### 3.5 Stability Analysis of the Crankâ€“Nicolson Method **Step 1:** Substituting the Fourier mode $G^j e^{\,i \xi i}$ into the Crankâ€“Nicolson formula and dividing by $G^j e^{\,i \xi i}$: $$-r e^{-i\xi} + (2 + 2r) - r e^{\,i\xi} = \frac{1}{G}\left[r e^{-i\xi} + (2 - 2r) + r e^{\,i\xi}\right]$$ **Step 2:** Using $e^{i\xi} + e^{-i\xi} = 2\cos\xi$: $$2 + 2r - 2r\cos\xi = \frac{1}{G}\left[2 - 2r + 2r\cos\xi\right]$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $G$: $$G = \frac{2 - 2r + 2r\cos\xi}{2 + 2r - 2r\cos\xi} = \frac{(2 - 2r) + 2r\cos\xi}{(2 + 2r) - 2r\cos\xi}$$ Or equivalently: $$G = \frac{1 - r(1 - \cos\xi)}{1 + r(1 - \cos\xi)}$$ **Step 4:** Since $1 - r(1 - \cos\xi) \le 1 + r(1 - \cos\xi)$ for all $r \ge 0$ and $\cos\xi \in [-1, 1]$, we have $\vert G \vert \le 1$ **unconditionally**. Thus Crankâ€“Nicolson is **unconditionally stable**. ### 3.6 Convergence Theorem (Lax Equivalence Theorem) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lax Equivalence Theorem:** For a consistent finite-difference scheme applied to a well-posed linear initial-value problem, **stability is equivalent to convergence**. That is, the method converges to the true solution if and only if it is stable. Since the three methods above are all consistent with the heat equation, stability implies convergence. For the explicit method, convergence holds only when $r \le 1/2$. The implicit and Crankâ€“Nicolson methods are convergent for any positive $r$. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Stencil Diagram â€” Computational Molecule of Each Method The following Mermaid flowchart shows the stencil of grid points used by each method. Filled nodes represent known values; hollow nodes represent the unknown to be computed. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Explicit FTCS]:::explicit --> B[3 points at level j known
Compute 1 point at level j+1] C[Implicit BTCS]:::implicit --> D[3 points at level j+1 coupled
Solve tridiagonal system] E[Crank Nicolson]:::cn --> F[3 points at level j AND 3 points at level j+1
Solve tridiagonal system] B --> G[Fully explicit formula
Stability r le 1 over 2] D --> H[Tridiagonal matrix A
Unconditionally stable] F --> I[Tridiagonal matrix A
Unconditionally stable and second order in time] classDef explicit fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef implicit fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cn fill:#d8bfd8,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Solving the Heat Equation Numerically ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Input Boundary Conditions
u0,t and uN,t]:::input P1[Input Initial Condition
ui,0 for all i]:::input P2[Choose Method
Explicit Implicit or CN]:::process P3[Choose Step Sizes
h and k Compute r]:::process P0 --> P4[Initialize Grid u i j] P1 --> P4 P3 --> P2 P2 --> P4 P4 --> Q1{Method Type?} Q1 -- Explicit --> Q2[Apply explicit formula
ui,j+1 equals r ui-1,j plus 1-2r ui,j plus r ui+1,j] Q1 -- Implicit --> Q3[Build tridiagonal matrix A
Solve A U j+1 equals U j
via Thomas algorithm] Q1 -- Crank Nicolson --> Q4[Build tridiagonal matrix A
Solve A U j+1 equals B U j
via Thomas algorithm] Q2 --> R1{Check stability
r le 1 over 2} R1 -- Yes --> S1[Advance j to j+1] R1 -- No --> X1[STOP Adjust h or k] X1 --> P3 Q3 --> S1 Q4 --> S1 S1 --> T1{All time steps done?} T1 -- No --> Q1 T1 -- Yes --> U1[Output Final Grid u i j
Plot or tabulate] classDef input fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#9370db,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Decision Flow â€” Method Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Need to solve parabolic PDE] --> B{Is the problem stiff
very small h required?} B -- No, moderate h okay --> C[Use Explicit Method
Simple and fast per step] B -- Yes, small h needed --> D{Is second order accuracy
in time required?} D -- No, first order okay --> E[Use Implicit Method
Unconditionally stable] D -- Yes, high accuracy needed --> F[Use Crank Nicolson Method
Best overall balance] C --> G[Constraint: r less than or equal to 0.5] E --> H[Cost: solve tridiagonal system each step] F --> I[Cost: solve tridiagonal system each step
plus more computation per step] classDef ext fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef imp fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cni fill:#d8bfd8,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Tridiagonal Matrix Structure (Implicit and Crankâ€“Nicolson) ``` For N = 5 interior points, the coefficient matrix A has the structure: [ 1+2r -r 0 0 0 ] [ u_{1,j+1} ] [ u_{1,j} + rÂ·u_{0,j+1} ] [ -r 1+2r -r 0 0 ] [ u_{2,j+1} ] [ u_{2,j} ] [ 0 -r 1+2r -r 0 ] Â· [ u_{3,j+1} ] = [ u_{3,j} ] [ 0 0 -r 1+2r -r ] [ u_{4,j+1} ] [ u_{4,j} ] [ 0 0 0 -r 1+2r ] [ u_{5,j+1} ] [ u_{5,j} + rÂ·u_{6,j+1} ] (Note: for Crankâ€“Nicolson, replace 1+2r with 2+2r and adjust RHS accordingly) ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam â€“ Model Question]** Define a parabolic partial differential equation. Give an example of a physical phenomenon that it models. **Model Answer:** A **parabolic partial differential equation** is a second-order PDE of the form $A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xt} + C u_{tt} + D u_x + E u_t + F = 0$ satisfying the discriminant condition $B^2 - 4AC = 0$. The classical example is the **one-dimensional heat equation**: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ It models the conduction of heat in a rod, where $u(x,t)$ is the temperature at position $x$ and time $t$, and $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity. It also models mass diffusion, groundwater transport, and financial option pricing (Blackâ€“Scholes equation). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam â€“ Model Question]** What is the stability condition for the explicit finite-difference scheme applied to the heat equation? Why does it arise? **Model Answer:** The explicit (FTCS) scheme is stable **only if the mesh ratio parameter satisfies:** $$r = \frac{\alpha \, \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2} \le \frac{1}{2}$$ It arises from the **Von Neumann stability analysis**. Substituting a Fourier error mode $G^j e^{i\beta x}$ into the scheme gives the amplification factor: $$G = 1 - 2r(1 - \cos\theta), \quad \theta = \beta \Delta x$$ The condition $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for all $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ reduces to $r \le 1/2$. Violating this bound makes $\vert G \vert > 1$, causing the numerical error to grow without bound (the solution "explodes"). --- ### PART B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** > - **Do NOT confuse the stability condition** â€” many students incorrectly write $r < 1$ or $r \le 1$. The exact bound is $\mathbf{r \le 0.5}$. > - **Do NOT forget the boundary values** when forming the tridiagonal system â€” they must be moved to the RHS. > - **Do NOT mix up the formulas** for Implicit and Crankâ€“Nicolson. In Implicit, the LHS diagonal coefficient is $\mathbf{(1+2r)}$; in Crankâ€“Nicolson, it is $\mathbf{(2+2r)}$. > - Always show the **stability analysis (Von Neumann)** when explicitly asked â€” it carries significant marks. > - Compute the amplification factor $G$ explicitly and verify $\vert G \vert \le 1$. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) â€” Explicit Method Focus** **(a)** Solve the heat equation $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ with $u(0,t) = u(1,t) = 0$ and $u(x,0) = \sin(\pi x)$, using the **explicit method** with $h = 0.5$ and $k = 0.0625$. Compute $u$ for **one time step** (i.e., compute $u_{i,1}$). **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** State the **Von Neumann stability condition** for the explicit scheme and verify whether the chosen $h$ and $k$ satisfy it. **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a)** With $h = 0.5$, $k = 0.0625$, and $\alpha = 1$: $$r = \frac{\alpha k}{h^2} = \frac{0.0625}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{0.0625}{0.25} = 0.25$$ The grid points are $x_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 0.5$, $x_2 = 1.0$. Initial values: $u_{0,0} = 0$, $u_{1,0} = \sin(\pi \cdot 0.5) = 1$, $u_{2,0} = 0$. Explicit formula: $$u_{i,j+1} = r \cdot u_{i-1,j} + (1 - 2r) \cdot u_{i,j} + r \cdot u_{i+1,j}$$ For $i = 1$ (the only interior point): $$u_{1,1} = (0.25)(0) + (1 - 0.5)(1) + (0.25)(0) = 0.5$$ So the temperature at $x = 0.5$ after one time step is $u_{1,1} = 0.5$. The boundary values remain: $u_{0,1} = 0$, $u_{2,1} = 0$. | $i$ | $x_i$ | $u_{i,0}$ | $u_{i,1}$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0.0 | 0 | 0 | | 1 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 0.5 | | 2 | 1.0 | 0 | 0 | --- **(b)** Von Neumann Stability Analysis for the Explicit Method: Assume the error has the form $\varepsilon_{i,j} = G^j e^{i \beta x_i}$. Substituting into the explicit scheme: $$G^{j+1} e^{i\beta x_i} = r G^j e^{i\beta x_{i-1}} + (1-2r) G^j e^{i\beta x_i} + r G^j e^{i\beta x_{i+1}}$$ Dividing by $G^j e^{i\beta x_i}$: $$G = r e^{-i\beta h} + (1-2r) + r e^{i\beta h}$$ Using $e^{i\beta h} + e^{-i\beta h} = 2\cos(\beta h)$, and letting $\theta = \beta h$: $$G = 1 - 2r + 2r\cos\theta = 1 - 2r(1 - \cos\theta)$$ For stability: $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for all $\theta \in [0, \pi]$. The most restrictive case is $\theta = \pi$ (giving $\cos\theta = -1$): $$G_{\min} = 1 - 2r(1 - (-1)) = 1 - 4r$$ Requiring $G_{\min} \ge -1$: $$1 - 4r \ge -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4r \le 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r \le \frac{1}{2}$$ So the **stability condition is $r \le 1/2$**, equivalently $r \le 0.5$. **Verification:** Here $r = 0.25 < 0.5$. âś… **The chosen step sizes are STABLE.** --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) â€” Crankâ€“Nicolson Method Focus** **(a)** Derive the **Crankâ€“Nicolson finite-difference formula** for the 1-D heat equation $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$, clearly defining the mesh ratio $r$. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Apply the **Crankâ€“Nicolson method** to the heat equation $u_t = u_{xx}$ on $0 \le x \le 1$ with $u(0,t) = u(1,t) = 0$, $u(x,0) = \sin(\pi x)$, using $h = 0.5$ and $k = 0.0625$. Compute $u_{1,1}$. **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Derivation of the Crankâ€“Nicolson formula:** The 1-D heat equation is: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ We use a **forward time difference** and a **central space difference averaged at $j$ and $j+1$**: $$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} = \frac{\alpha}{2}\left[\frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} + \frac{u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}}{h^2}\right]$$ Let $r = \dfrac{\alpha k}{h^2}$. Multiply through by $2$: $$2u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} = r(u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}) + r(u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1})$$ Rearranging to put all $j+1$ terms on the left: $$-r \cdot u_{i-1,j+1} + (2 + 2r) \cdot u_{i,j+1} - r \cdot u_{i+1,j+1} = r \cdot u_{i-1,j} + (2 - 2r) \cdot u_{i,j} + r \cdot u_{i+1,j}$$ This is the **Crankâ€“Nicolson formula**. It requires solving a tridiagonal system at each time step, and is second-order accurate in both $k$ and $h$ (i.e., $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$), and is **unconditionally stable**. --- **(b) Application of Crankâ€“Nicolson Method:** With $h = 0.5$, $k = 0.0625$, $\alpha = 1$: $$r = \frac{k}{h^2} = \frac{0.0625}{0.25} = 0.25$$ Grid points: $x_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 0.5$, $x_2 = 1.0$. Initial condition: $u_{0,0} = 0$, $u_{1,0} = \sin(\pi/2) = 1$, $u_{2,0} = 0$. For the single interior point $i = 1$, the Crankâ€“Nicolson formula becomes: $$-r \cdot u_{0,1} + (2+2r) \cdot u_{1,1} - r \cdot u_{2,1} = r \cdot u_{0,0} + (2-2r) \cdot u_{1,0} + r \cdot u_{2,0}$$ Substituting known values and $r = 0.25$: $$-(0.25)(0) + (2 + 0.5) \cdot u_{1,1} - (0.25)(0) = (0.25)(0) + (2 - 0.5)(1) + (0.25)(0)$$ $$2.5 \cdot u_{1,1} = 1.5$$ $$u_{1,1} = \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.6$$ So the temperature at $x = 0.5$ after one time step using Crankâ€“Nicolson is $u_{1,1} = 0.6$. (Note: Compare with the exact solution $u(0.5, 0.0625) = e^{-\pi^2 (0.0625)}\sin(\pi/2) = e^{-0.6169} \approx 0.5396$. Crankâ€“Nicolson gives a more accurate result ($0.6$) than the explicit method ($0.5$) at this coarse grid.) --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - **Parabolic PDE** = $B^2 - 4AC = 0$; canonical example = **1-D Heat Equation** $u_t = \alpha u_{xx}$. - **Mesh ratio** $r = \alpha k / h^2$ â€” this single parameter controls everything (accuracy and stability). - **Three methods compared:** - **Explicit (FTCS)**: $u_{i,j+1} = r u_{i-1,j} + (1-2r) u_{i,j} + r u_{i+1,j}$ â€” stable only if $r \le 0.5$, accuracy $O(k) + O(h^2)$. - **Implicit (BTCS)**: $-r u_{i-1,j+1} + (1+2r) u_{i,j+1} - r u_{i+1,j+1} = u_{i,j}$ â€” unconditionally stable, accuracy $O(k) + O(h^2)$. - **Crankâ€“Nicolson**: $-r u_{i-1,j+1} + (2+2r) u_{i,j+1} - r u_{i+1,j+1} = r u_{i-1,j} + (2-2r) u_{i,j} + r u_{i+1,j}$ â€” unconditionally stable, accuracy $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$ â€” **best overall method**. - **Von Neumann Stability** technique: substitute $\varepsilon_{i,j} = G^j e^{i\beta x_i}$, find $G$, demand $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for all $\theta = \beta h \in [0, \pi]$. - **Lax Equivalence Theorem**: for consistent schemes on well-posed problems, **stability $\Leftrightarrow$ convergence**. - **Implicit & Crankâ€“Nicolson** both require solving a **tridiagonal system** at each time step (use **Thomas algorithm**). - **Always** move known boundary values $u_{0,j+1}$ and $u_{N,j+1}$ from LHS to RHS before solving the tridiagonal system. - **Crankâ€“Nicolson is the preferred method** for parabolic problems in real engineering applications due to its second-order accuracy in both time and space and unconditional stability.

topics.title: Hyperbolic Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET833 - NUMERICAL TECHNIQUES ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hyperbolic Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hyperbolic Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** is a second-order linear PDE of the form:

$$A \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + B \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial y} + C \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + D \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + E \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + F u + G = 0$$

where the **discriminant** satisfies the condition $\mathbf{B^2 - 4AC > 0}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The canonical hyperbolic equation studied under the KTU 2024 Scheme OEMET833 syllabus is the **one-dimensional wave equation**, given by $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$, where $c$ is the **wave propagation velocity** (in m/s) and $u(x,t)$ represents the wave displacement.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy â€” A Vibrating Guitar String**
>
> Imagine plucking a guitar string. The string doesn't just pop to the displaced position; instead, a **disturbance travels along its length** in both directions at a finite speed $c$. The shape of the string at any moment is governed by the **wave equation**.
>
> - **Why "hyperbolic"?** The characteristic curves (along which solutions propagate) form a family of **hyperbolas** in the $x-t$ plane, much like the geometric shape $x^2/a^2 - t^2/b^2 = 1$.
> - **Key difference from parabolic/elliptic equations:** Hyperbolic equations model *propagating waves* â€” information travels along characteristics, not instantly across the domain.

### 1.3 Classification of Second-Order PDEs

| Discriminant Condition | Equation Type | Canonical Form | Physical Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $B^2 - 4AC < 0$ | Elliptic | Laplace / Poisson | Steady-state heat |
| $B^2 - 4AC = 0$ | Parabolic | Heat / Diffusion | Transient heat flow |
| $B^2 - 4AC > 0$ | **Hyperbolic** | **Wave equation** | **Vibrating string** |

### 1.4 Why Study Hyperbolic Equations in Engineering?

- **Structural Engineering:** Seismic wave propagation through soil and buildings.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Transmission line equations (telegraph equations).
- **Acoustics:** Sound wave propagation in air and fluids.
- **Aerospace:** Shock wave analysis in supersonic flows.
- **Telecommunications:** Signal propagation in optical fibers and waveguides.
- **Geophysics:** Earthquake and tsunami modeling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The One-Dimensional Wave Equation

The governing PDE for a vibrating string with small transverse vibrations is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}, \quad 0 < x < L, \quad t > 0$$

**Standard auxiliary conditions:**

1. **Boundary Conditions (BCs):**
   - Fixed ends: $u(0, t) = 0$ and $u(L, t) = 0$
   - Free ends: $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(0, t) = 0$ and $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}(L, t) = 0$

2. **Initial Conditions (ICs):**
   - Initial displacement: $u(x, 0) = f(x)$
   - Initial velocity: $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}(x, 0) = g(x)$

> [!NOTE]
> **D'Alembert's Analytical Solution** is $u(x,t) = \frac{1}{2}[f(x+ct) + f(x-ct)] + \frac{1}{2c}\int_{x-ct}^{x+ct} g(\xi)\, d\xi$, representing two waves traveling in opposite directions.

### 2.2 Finite Difference Discretization

Let the domain be discretized as:
- $x_i = i \cdot \Delta x$, where $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$
- $t_j = j \cdot \Delta t$, where $j = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- $u_i^j \approx u(x_i, t_j)$

**Second-order central differences:**

$$\left.\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}\right|_{(i,j)} = \frac{u_i^{j+1} - 2u_i^j + u_i^{j-1}}{(\Delta t)^2} + O((\Delta t)^2)$$

$$\left.\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\right|_{(i,j)} = \frac{u_{i+1}^j - 2u_i^j + u_{i-1}^j}{(\Delta x)^2} + O((\Delta x)^2)$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Method** | **Explicit Finite Difference Formula** | **Stability** | **Truncation Error** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Explicit Scheme** | $u_i^{j+1} = 2u_i^j + r^2(u_{i+1}^j + u_{i-1}^j - 2u_i^j) - u_i^{j-1}$ | $r \leq 1$ | $O((\Delta t)^2 + (\Delta x)^2)$ |
| **Implicit Scheme** | $u_i^{j+1} = 2u_i^j + r^2(u_{i+1}^{j+1} - 2u_i^{j+1} + u_{i-1}^{j+1}) - u_i^{j-1}$ | Always stable | $O((\Delta t)^2 + (\Delta x)^2)$ |
| **Crank-Nicolson** | Average of explicit and implicit | Always stable | $O((\Delta t)^2 + (\Delta x)^2)$ |
| **Lax-Wendroff** | Two-step predictor-corrector | $r \leq 1$ | $O((\Delta t)^2 + (\Delta x)^2)$ |

where the **Courant Number** is defined as:

$$r = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta x}$$

### 2.4 Compact Form of the Explicit Formula

By letting $\lambda = r^2 = \left(\frac{c \, \Delta t}{\Delta x}\right)^2$, the explicit scheme becomes:

$$u_i^{j+1} = 2(1 - \lambda)\, u_i^j + \lambda \left( u_{i+1}^j + u_{i-1}^j \right) - u_i^{j-1}$$

### 2.5 First Time Step Initialization

The explicit formula requires **two previous time levels** ($j$ and $j-1$). To compute $u_i^1$, we use the initial velocity condition with a forward-time difference:

$$u_i^1 = u_i^0 + \Delta t \cdot g(x_i) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\left( u_{i+1}^0 - 2u_i^0 + u_{i-1}^0 \right)$$

### 2.6 CFL Stability Condition (Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CFL Condition:** For the explicit scheme to remain numerically stable:
>
> $$r = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1$$
>
> Violation of this condition causes spurious oscillations and divergence â€” a critical KTU exam point.

### 2.7 Boundary Treatment

- **Dirichlet BC:** $u_0^j = \alpha(t_j)$ and $u_n^j = \beta(t_j)$ (specified values).
- **Neumann BC:** $\frac{u_{n+1}^j - u_{n-1}^j}{2\Delta x} = \gamma(t_j)$ (specified derivatives).

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Seismic Analysis:** Engineers use explicit schemes to model earthquake wave propagation to design earthquake-resistant buildings.
- **Acoustic Damping:** Hyperbolic solvers are used in noise-canceling headphones and concert hall design.
- **Power Systems:** Lightning surge analysis in transmission lines uses telegraph equations (hyperbolic PDEs).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Explicit Finite Difference Scheme

**Step 1:** Start with the one-dimensional wave equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 2:** Approximate the time derivative using a second-order central difference in $t$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} \approx \frac{u_i^{j+1} - 2u_i^j + u_i^{j-1}}{(\Delta t)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Approximate the spatial derivative using a second-order central difference in $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \approx \frac{u_{i+1}^j - 2u_i^j + u_{i-1}^j}{(\Delta x)^2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the approximations into the wave equation:

$$\frac{u_i^{j+1} - 2u_i^j + u_i^{j-1}}{(\Delta t)^2} = c^2 \cdot \frac{u_{i+1}^j - 2u_i^j + u_{i-1}^j}{(\Delta x)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $(\Delta t)^2$:

$$u_i^{j+1} - 2u_i^j + u_i^{j-1} = c^2 \cdot \frac{(\Delta t)^2}{(\Delta x)^2} \left( u_{i+1}^j - 2u_i^j + u_{i-1}^j \right)$$

**Step 6:** Define the Courant number $r = \dfrac{c \, \Delta t}{\Delta x}$, so $\lambda = r^2$:

$$u_i^{j+1} - 2u_i^j + u_i^{j-1} = \lambda \left( u_{i+1}^j - 2u_i^j + u_{i-1}^j \right)$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $u_i^{j+1}$ (the unknown future value):

$$u_i^{j+1} = 2u_i^j - u_i^{j-1} + \lambda \left( u_{i+1}^j - 2u_i^j + u_{i-1}^j \right)$$

**Step 8:** Rearrange into the standard compact form:

$$\boxed{u_i^{j+1} = 2(1 - \lambda)\, u_i^j + \lambda \left( u_{i+1}^j + u_{i-1}^j \right) - u_i^{j-1}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the First Time Step ($j=0 \rightarrow j=1$)

The explicit formula needs $u_i^0$ and $u_i^1$ to compute $u_i^2$. To obtain $u_i^1$, we use the **initial velocity** $g(x)$:

**Step 1:** Forward difference in time at $t = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\bigg|_{t=0} \approx \frac{u_i^1 - u_i^0}{\Delta t} = g(x_i)$$

**Step 2:** Therefore $u_i^1 = u_i^0 + \Delta t \cdot g(x_i)$.

**Step 3:** Taylor series refinement (to maintain $O((\Delta t)^2)$ accuracy):

$$u(x, \Delta t) = u(x, 0) + \Delta t \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\bigg|_{0} + \frac{(\Delta t)^2}{2} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}\bigg|_{0} + O((\Delta t)^3)$$

**Step 4:** Replace $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}$ with $c^2 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$:

$$u_i^1 = u_i^0 + \Delta t \cdot g(x_i) + \frac{(\Delta t)^2}{2} \cdot c^2 \cdot \frac{u_{i+1}^0 - 2u_i^0 + u_{i-1}^0}{(\Delta x)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\lambda = c^2 (\Delta t)^2 / (\Delta x)^2$:

$$\boxed{u_i^1 = u_i^0 + \Delta t \cdot g(x_i) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\left( u_{i+1}^0 - 2u_i^0 + u_{i-1}^0 \right)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example â€” KTU-Style Numerical Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Problem:** Solve the wave equation $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = 4 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ with $c = 2$, $L = 4$, $\Delta x = 1$, $\Delta t = 0.5$, BCs $u(0,t) = u(4,t) = 0$, ICs $u(x,0) = \sin(\pi x/4)$ and $u_t(x,0) = 0$. Compute $u$ for two time steps.

**Step 1:** Compute the Courant number:

$$r = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta x} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{1} = 1, \quad \lambda = r^2 = 1$$

Since $r = 1 \leq 1$, the scheme is **stable** (boundary case).

**Step 2:** Compute initial spatial values at $t = 0$:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $u_i^0 = \sin(\pi x_i / 4)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | 0.0000 |
| 1 | 1 | $\sin(\pi/4) \approx 0.7071$ |
| 2 | 2 | $\sin(\pi/2) = 1.0000$ |
| 3 | 3 | $\sin(3\pi/4) \approx 0.7071$ |
| 4 | 4 | $\sin(\pi) = 0.0000$ |

**Step 3:** Compute $u_i^1$ using the first time step formula (since $g(x_i) = 0$):

$$u_i^1 = u_i^0 + \frac{1}{2}\left( u_{i+1}^0 - 2u_i^0 + u_{i-1}^0 \right)$$

- $u_0^1 = 0$ (boundary)
- $u_1^1 = 0.7071 + 0.5 \times (1.0000 - 1.4142 + 0.0000) = 0.7071 + 0.5 \times (-0.4142) = 0.7071 - 0.2071 = 0.5000$
- $u_2^1 = 1.0000 + 0.5 \times (0.7071 - 2.0000 + 0.7071) = 1.0000 + 0.5 \times (-0.5858) = 1.0000 - 0.2929 = 0.7071$
- $u_3^1 = 0.7071 + 0.5 \times (0.0000 - 1.4142 + 1.0000) = 0.7071 + 0.5 \times (-0.4142) = 0.7071 - 0.2071 = 0.5000$
- $u_4^1 = 0$ (boundary)

**Step 4:** Compute $u_i^2$ using the main explicit formula with $\lambda = 1$:

$$u_i^2 = 2(1-1)u_i^1 + 1 \cdot (u_{i+1}^1 + u_{i-1}^1) - u_i^0 = u_{i+1}^1 + u_{i-1}^1 - u_i^0$$

- $u_0^2 = 0$ (boundary)
- $u_1^2 = u_2^1 + u_0^1 - u_1^0 = 0.7071 + 0 - 0.7071 = 0.0000$
- $u_2^2 = u_3^1 + u_1^1 - u_2^0 = 0.5000 + 0.5000 - 1.0000 = 0.0000$
- $u_3^2 = u_4^1 + u_2^1 - u_3^0 = 0 + 0.7071 - 0.7071 = 0.0000$
- $u_4^2 = 0$ (boundary)

**Step 5:** Verify with the analytical solution. Since $c = 2$ and $g(x) = 0$:

$$u(x,t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[ \sin\left(\frac{\pi(x+2t)}{4}\right) + \sin\left(\frac{\pi(x-2t)}{4}\right) \right] = \sin\left(\frac{\pi x}{4}\right)\cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{2}\right)$$

At $t = 1$ and $x = 1$:

$$u(1, 1) = \sin(\pi/4) \cos(\pi/2) = 0.7071 \times 0 = 0 \checkmark$$

The numerical solution **matches exactly** because the CFL condition $r = 1$ is at its optimal limit for this problem.

### 3.4 Truncation Error Analysis (Von Neumann Stability)

Substitute a Fourier mode $u_i^j = \xi^j e^{I k i \Delta x}$ (where $I = \sqrt{-1}$ and $k$ is the wavenumber) into the explicit scheme:

$$\xi^{j+1} e^{I k i \Delta x} = 2(1-\lambda) \xi^j e^{I k i \Delta x} + \lambda \xi^j \left( e^{I k (i+1) \Delta x} + e^{I k (i-1) \Delta x} \right) - \xi^{j-1} e^{I k i \Delta x}$$

Divide by $\xi^{j-1} e^{I k i \Delta x}$:

$$\xi^2 = 2(1-\lambda)\xi + \lambda \xi (e^{I k \Delta x} + e^{-I k \Delta x}) - 1$$

Using the identity $e^{I\theta} + e^{-I\theta} = 2\cos\theta$:

$$\xi^2 - 2\xi\left[ 1 - 2\lambda \sin^2\left(\frac{k \Delta x}{2}\right) \right] + 1 = 0$$

**Stability condition:** The product of roots must equal $1$ (characteristic equation for $\xi$). For $|\xi| \leq 1$ for all $k$:

$$\lambda \leq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \frac{c \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1$$

This **derives** the CFL stability condition rigorously.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Computational Stencil for the Explicit Scheme

The diagram below shows the **5-point stencil** used in the explicit finite difference scheme. Each value $u_i^{j+1}$ at the new time level depends on three points at the current time level $j$ and one point at the previous time level $j-1$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["u_{i-1}^{j-1}"] --> D["Explicit Scheme"]
    B["u_{i}^{j-1}"] --> D
    C["u_{i+1}^{j-1}"] --> D
    E["u_{i-1}^{j}"] --> D
    F["u_{i}^{j}"] --> D
    G["u_{i+1}^{j}"] --> D
    D --> H["u_{i}^{j+1}"]
    
    style A fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#C1E1FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#C1E1FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hyperbolic PDE Solver

```mermaid
graph TD
    INP1["Input Grid Mesh x_i, t_j"] --> PROC1
    INP2["Initial Conditions u(x,0) and u_t(x,0)"] --> PROC1
    INP3["Boundary Conditions"] --> PROC1
    INP4["Parameter c, dx, dt, lambda"] --> PROC1
    
    PROC1["Initialize u_i^0 from f(x)"] --> PROC2
    PROC2["Stability Check r = c dt / dx <= 1"] --> DECCHK{Stable?}
    
    DECCHK -->|No| ERR["ERROR Unstable Scheme"]
    DECCHK -->|Yes| PROC3
    
    PROC3["Compute First Step u_i^1"] --> PROC4
    PROC4["Apply Explicit Formula u_i^{j+1} = 2(1-lambda)u_i^j + lambda(u_{i+1}^j + u_{i-1}^j) - u_i^{j-1}"] --> PROC5
    PROC5["Apply Boundary Conditions at i=0 and i=n"] --> DECLOOP{j <= N?}
    
    DECLOOP -->|Yes| PROC4
    DECLOOP -->|No| OUT
    
    ERR --> ENDNODE["Stop"]
    OUT["Output Full Solution u_i^j for all j"] --> VIZ["Visualization Wave Propagation"]
    VIZ --> ENDNODE
    
    style PROC1 fill:#C1E1FF,stroke:#333
    style PROC2 fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333
    style PROC3 fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333
    style PROC4 fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333
    style PROC5 fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333
    style DECCHK fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#333
    style DECLOOP fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#333
    style ERR fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333
    style ENDNODE fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Method Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["Wave Equation d2u/dt2 = c2 d2u/dx2"] --> CHOOSE{Choose Method}
    
    CHOOSE -->|Explicit| EX["Direct 3-point stencil at time j and j-1"]
    CHOOSE -->|Implicit| IM["Solve Tridiagonal System Each Step"]
    CHOOSE -->|Crank-Nicolson| CN["Average of Explicit and Implicit"]
    CHOOSE -->|Lax-Wendroff| LW["Two-step Predictor Corrector"]
    
    EX --> EXS{Stability r <= 1}
    EXS -->|Satisfied| EXOK["Fast Computation"]
    EXS -->|Violated| EXFAIL["Numerical Blow-up"]
```
